EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong high pressure and very dry air mass resulted in abnormally hot daytime temperatures across all of southern Arizona.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Well above normal daytime temperatures settled over southern Arizona on June 27 and 28. On June 27th, a record 112 degrees was recorded at the Tucson ASOS. Several valley locations below four thousand feet reached heat criteria levels. Heat criteria was also reached in the higher elevations. The Chiricahua National Monument (5133 ft) recorded a high temperature of 102 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Long term drought conditions continued in July with some improvement due to beneficial monsoon rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: July brought much needed monsoon moisture to southern Arizona, but rainfall amounts were not sufficient to drastically reduce the severity of the drought conditions. Drought conditions were unchanged to start the month with some reduction of D3 and D4 conditions by the end of the month. The Tucson area was downgraded from D3 to D2 conditions as well as portions of western Pima County, southeast Pinal County, and northwest Graham County. Extreme southwest Cochise County was downgraded from D4 conditions to D3 conditions. Most areas ended the month with below normal rainfall, with only portions of western Cochise County and northern Greenlee County experiencing above normal rainfall. Above normal rainfall over the Monument Fire area resulted in significant flash flooding and additional damage to the area. According to state officials, range conditions continued to be extremely poor with dry stock tanks. Water was being hauled to fill stock ponds and tanks, and feed had to be brought in to supplement poor forage conditions. Wildlife was also being impacted due to lack of water and available forage. The Arizona Drought Watch reported severe impacts in the Upper Gila River Valley in southern Greenlee County. Unusually low water levels were observed in reservoirs, lakes, and ponds. Poor water quality due to low levels and low flows were also reported. Cattle were being supplemented, but herds were still losing fat levels.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large dust devil moved through the community of Desert Hills in north Phoenix and caused damage to one of the homes in the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 2 pm on May 13, a huge dust devil passed over a home in Desert Hills, located between Anthem and Cave Creek in north Phoenix. The dust devil tore a number of shingles off of the roof, and flipped a swingset into their barn. According to the homeowner, the dust devil sounded like a freight train running through the house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific storm system brought strong winds to the region on Wednesday April 6. Strong winds resulted in several localized dust storms, one of which caused an accident.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong afternoon winds resulted in a dust storm along Interstate 10 between Red Rock and Picacho. A three vehicle accident was reported just north of Red Rock. One of the vehicles was a jack-knifed tractor-trailer. Northbound lanes were closed for approximately one hour and the interstate was backed up for several miles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Long term drought continued into March resulting in an upgrade to D3 conditions across southeast Arizona.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Abnormally dry conditions continued during the month of March. The southeast quarter of the state received less than 25% of average rainfall year-to-date and less than 50% for the water year (October to September). This lack of rainfall resulted in an upgrade from D2 conditions to D3 conditions across much of Cochise County on March 8. Later in the month on March 29, all of Cochise County as well as Santa Cruz County, southern Graham County, southern Greenlee County, and eastern Pima County were upgraded to D3 conditions. Prolonged drought conditions helped worsen critical fire weather conditions which helped contribute to numerous small brush fires. The Arizona Drought Watch report for extreme southeast Cochise County, including the Rio Bavispe watershed, found many ponds that normally have water during March were dry. Also, intermittent streams that normally flow were dry in many or all segments. Little to no green forage existed in the grasses or forbs, and pipelines and troughs have replaced most pond water resulting in significant agriculture impacts. The Arizona Drought Watch report for extreme southeast Pima County, including the Santa Cruz River watershed, found that drought impacts are present and severe. The flow in the Santa Cruz is less than last year and about half the average flow seen within drought years. Rising severity was also observed. Water levels were approximately two feet lower than last year and approxiamately six feet lower than pre-drought levels.

CZ_NAME: UPPER GILA RIVER AND ARAVAIPA VALLEYS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe to extreme drought conditions continued through the month of April across southeast Arizona.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dry conditions continued during the month of April across much of Arizona as only one significant winter storm moved through the state. Portions of southeast Cochise County received no measurable rainfall. Portions of south central Arizona received amounts from one tenth to one half inch while other areas, including eastern mountain ranges, received one tenth of an inch or less. More than 27,000 acres in Arizona have been consumed by wildfires through the end of April, mostly in the southern part of the state. Little change in the D3 condition area with all of Cochise and Santa Cruz Counties under D3 conditions. Portions of southern Greenlee, southern Graham, and eastern Pima were also still under D3 conditions. ||Arizona Drought Watch reported several impacts during the month. The Santa Cruz River basin of eastern Pima county reported unusually low flows in streams, rivers, and springs. Low soil moisture was resulting in plant stress, newly observed plant mortality, and oak leaves browning and falling with only 25% of Oaks flowering. Other unusual plant phenology was also observed with pines not producing cones and most flowers not budding at all. Dead oaks and ponderosas were quickly rotting at the base and falling within several months.||In the San Pedro River Basin in northern Cochise and southern Graham Counties, Arizona Drought Watch reported plant stress due to unusually low soil moisture. Native plants, including cacti and cotton plants are stressed, or have died.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered high based thunderstorms developed across southwestern Arizona, including the Yuma area, during the evening hours on August 27th. The storms generated gusty wind in excess of 58 mph which produced some damage across portions of Yuma.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered strong thunderstorms moved across the community of Yuma during the late evening hours on August 27th. The storms produced gusty and damaging winds estimated to be in excess of 60 mph. According to Yuma law enforcement personnel, numerous large trees and power poles were blown down across the city. No injuries were reported due to the damaging winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoon moisture and an upper level low fueled thunderstorms over the Mojave Desert.  Several storms produced severe weather and flash flooding.
Extreme drought conditions continued across southern Arizona in November.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After a dry October, D3 (extreme) drought conditions spread across much of southern Arizona to begin November. That marked an increase from 13.88% of the state to 28.59% under D3 conditions. That's the largest total area for D3 conditions since the drought began last winter. There was some relief the first half of the month with a couple of storm systems bringing very beneficial rainfall. Tucson and Douglas ended the month with above normal rainfall, but a majority of southern Arizona ended below normal, including the White Mountain region. Though shower activity in November brought temporary relief, the Arizona State Climatology Office reported that it was too late in the season to help range conditions impacted by the spotty rainfall and hot conditions from the past summer. The light nature of the rainfall also did very little to help rangeland water resources, including ponds and streams. However, rainfall will help winter annuals and crops.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Wallow Fire started on May 29, 2011 from an abandoned campfire in the Bear Wallow Wilderness in northern Greenlee County about 24 miles southwest of Alpine, Arizona. The fire spread into Apache County on June first. Red Flag conditions caused fire spreads of 8 and 12 miles on different days. This fire eventually burned 538,049 total acres in eastern Arizona and western New Mexico (15,397 acres in New Mexico). In Apache County, 313,103 acres burned in Apache County (WFO Flagstaff's CWA). Containment (the fire did not spread any more) was achieved by the end of June and the fire was controlled (declared out) on July 13th. Over 6,000 people were employed to fight this fire at a cost of over $100,000,000! It became the largest fire in Arizona history. In all, there were 32 residences destroyed, 5 residences damaged, 4 commercial properties destroyed, 36 outbuildings destroyed, 1 damaged outbuilding, and one vehicle destroyed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Wallow Fire entered Apache County around 200 PM on June first. The fire burned a total of 313,103 acres in Apache County. The fire did not spread after June 30th (it was contained). The fire was declared out (controlled) on July 13th. It cost an estimated 58 million dollars to fight the fire in Apache County alone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms developed across portions of south central Arizona, including the greater Phoenix area, during the late afternoon and evening hours on August 18th. The storms produced strong gusty outflow winds which caused a dust storm over the lower deserts, including portions of the greater Phoenix area and western Pinal counties. Damaging microburst winds and locally heavy rain accompanied the strong storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Intense thunderstorms developed over portions of central Pinal county during the afternoon hours on August 18th. The storms produced strong and gusty outflow winds in excess of 50 mph which moved towards the northwest and generated a significant haboob. The haboob moved from the Eloy/Casa Grande/Coolidge area towards the northwest and into the greater Phoenix metropolitan area. The haboob was at least 2000 feet high and 50 miles wide. Between 4 and 430 pm, several trained spotters in the Coolidge and Casa Grande area reported a dust storm with visibilities at or below one quarter mile. At 5 pm, zero visibility in dense blowing dust was reported along the Hunt Highway in the San Tan valley. The haboob significantly disrupted the rush hour traffic across northwest Pinal county. Fortunately no major accidents or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Long term drought conditions continued during October with very little improvement during the month due to very little rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Following near normal Monsoon rainfall, dry and warm conditions returned to southern Arizona in October. Most areas received less than 50 percent of October's normal rainfall with a large swath receiving less than 10 percent of normal rainfall. Tucson International Airport received six hundredths of an inch for the month, while the worse drought areas of southeast Cochise County received up to fifteen hundredths of an inch. As a result, only enough precipitation fell to prevent conditions from getting much worse during the month. The lone positive was slight improvement in Greenlee County where D4 (exceptional) drought conditions dropped from 1.67 percent of the state in September to 1.24 percent in October. Local drought impacts were somewhat mitigated as the drought has persisted for several months. However, with the help of irrigation and normal Monsoon rainfall, the fall cotton crop has been reported to be fair to excellent.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated high based thunderstorms developed in the Wickenburg area during the late afternoon hours, and the storms generated strong, gusty and damaging winds. Peak wind gusts were estimated to be near 70 mph with the stronger storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Isolated high based thunderstorms developed in the Wickenburg area during the late afternoon hours on August 26th. The storms generated strong gusty winds, estimated by local law enforcement to be upwards of 70 mph. The strong winds downed several power poles which caused the area near North Vulture Mine Road and Easy Street to be closed to traffic.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe thunderstorm development had occured in the early afternoon hours of July 5th near the Tucson, AZ area and east of Tucson. As thunderstorms dissipated and began moving northwest, outflows from these storms began producing very strong downburst winds, some in excess of 70 mph. Around 6:30pm MST, the NWS Phoenix office began receiving calls of dust moving into the Casa Grande, AZ area, about 50 miles southeast of downtown Phoenix. Upon examining radar data, it is estimated the dust storm reached a maximum height of 5000 feet to 6000 feet, the aerial coverage was close to 100 miles and the distance traveled was around 150 miles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with damaging microburst winds moved across the town of Buckeye during the evening hours on July 5th. Wind gusts estimated to be at least 60 knots ripped away a 5 foot wide section of the roof of Youngker high school in Buckeye. According to Jeff Simmons, Buckeye Union High School District business manager, falling debris shattered at least 2 windows on the adjacent administrative building. After the roof was damaged, rain damaged 10 classrooms at the school. Further to the south, the microburst winds toppled the Buckeye Police Department's 60 foot communications tower, crushing a shaded parking structure near the police offices. Finally, 30 trees near the Youngker campus were damaged by the thunderstorms. The school district's insurance company estimate roof repairs could reach 500 thousand dollars. A new communications tower will cost approximately 100 thousand dollars.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms developed across the south central deserts of Arizona during the late afternoon and evening hours on August 26th. The storms produced strong gusty outflow winds which were strong enough to generate dust storm conditions over the greater Phoenix metropolitan area, as well as the deserts of northwestern Pinal county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms developed across the south central deserts during the late afternoon hours on August 26th, and they generated strong gusty winds which led to dust storm conditions over portions of northwest Pinal county. Multiple trained spotters in the community of Casa Grande reported visibilities at or below one quarter mile in dense blowing dust. One of the spotters in Casa Grande reported 50 foot visibility in a dust storm. The sharply reduced visibility was a major disruption to area motorists during the rush hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms developed across portions of south central Arizona, including the greater Phoenix area, during the late afternoon and evening hours on August 18th. The storms produced strong gusty outflow winds which caused a dust storm over the lower deserts, including portions of the greater Phoenix area and western Pinal counties. Damaging microburst winds and locally heavy rain accompanied the strong storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with damaging microburst winds affected the area around Magma during the afternoon hours on August 18th. According to a report from the local utility company, approximately 30 power poles were blown down in the Johnson Ranch area, along the Hunt highway near Judd road. Winds were estimated to be in excess of 50 knots.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced large hail and gusty winds in Snowflake.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter in Snowflake reported 1 1/4 inch diameter hail and wind gusts of 35 to 40 MPH from 1416 to 1426 MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced between 0.16 and 1.54 inches of rain around the Schultz Fire burned area. Some rain gages reported more than an inch of rain in just 15 minutes. This produced the worst flooding of the 2011 Monsoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flash flooding in many of the neighborhoods below the Schultz Fire burn area.  Three primary roads were closed and most roads in the affected area were impassable. Water was running 35 feet wide and two feet deep through one residential property.  A County Declaration of Emergency was signed in response to this flooding. Portions of rock filled wire baskets that lined one drainage canal collapsed into the the canal and had to be removed and replace.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure center over northern Arizona and abundant moisture brought thunderstorms with heavy rain and severe weather to the northern portions of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dilkon Police Officers reported 2 inch diameter hail 3 miles west-southwest of Dilkon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure center over northern Arizona and abundant moisture brought thunderstorms with heavy rain and severe weather to the northern portions of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dilkon Police Officers reported a brief Funnel Cloud with possible touch down (tornado).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cut off low over southern California transported abundant moisture,  increasing sheer, and cape over northern Arizona.  This lead to strong and a few severe thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A flash flood 6 feet deep flowed out of Phantom Canyon and into Bright Angel Creek/Canyon.  The creeks were rearranged, boulders were moved, trees were ripped from the banks of Phantom Creek and deposited at the intersection of Bright Angel Creek. This water then flowed into the Colorado River in the Grand Canyon. This was the highest flood in Phantom and Bright Angel Creeks since 1997.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extreme drought conditions continued across southern Arizona in November.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After a dry October, D3 (extreme) drought conditions spread across much of southern Arizona to begin November. That marked an increase from 13.88% of the state to 28.59% under D3 conditions. That's the largest total area for D3 conditions since the drought began last winter. There was some relief the first half of the month with a couple of storm systems bringing very beneficial rainfall. Tucson and Douglas ended the month with above normal rainfall, but a majority of southern Arizona ended below normal, including the White Mountain region. Though shower activity in November brought temporary relief, the Arizona State Climatology Office reported that it was too late in the season to help range conditions impacted by the spotty rainfall and hot conditions from the past summer. The light nature of the rainfall also did very little to help rangeland water resources, including ponds and streams. However, rainfall will help winter annuals and crops.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rain produced flash flooding in the area where the Wallow Fire burned.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotters reported water, mud, and burn area debris/ash flowing across residential properties and threatening homes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rain produced flash flooding in the area where the Wallow Fire burned.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused Colter Creek to overflow the banks and flood yards. One quarter of an inch of rain fell in just 15 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rain produced flash flooding in areas that have burned in the past 13 months.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several intersections along Campbell Avenue were impassable due to small debris, rocks, with shallow surface water overflowing intended drainages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rain produced flash flooding in areas that have burned in the past 13 months.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Colter Creek in Nutrioso was flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms developed across portions of south central Arizona, including the greater Phoenix area, during the late afternoon and evening hours on August 18th. The storms produced strong gusty outflow winds which caused a dust storm over the lower deserts, including portions of the greater Phoenix area and western Pinal counties. Damaging microburst winds and locally heavy rain accompanied the strong storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms with damaging microburst winds moved through the San Tan Valley during the late afternoon hours on August 18th. Area law enforcement personnel reported that power lines were down at Bella Vista and Gantzel roads, and at least one of the lines fell onto a school bus from the Florence Unified school district. The bus driver and four children that were aboard were not injured.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoon moisture caused scattered thunderstorms across northern Arizona caused heavy rain, flash flooding,  a rock slide, and a funnel cloud.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Department of Public Safety worker reported a funnel cloud northwest of Eager. Radar showed an approximate distance of 10 miles northwest of Eager.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoon moisture caused scattered thunderstorms across northern Arizona caused heavy rain, flash flooding,  a rock slide, and a funnel cloud.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Department of Public Safety reported a rockfall on Highway 89 about 4 miles northeast of Congress.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from thunderstorms produced flash flooding and debris flows on the west side of community of White River in and below the Locust Fire area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from a thunderstorm over the Locust Burn area produced significant debris flows and street flooding. Large rocks and sand were carried onto roadways and all drainages were full.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms developed across portions of south central Arizona, including the greater Phoenix area, during the late afternoon and evening hours on August 18th. The storms produced strong gusty outflow winds which caused a dust storm over the lower deserts, including portions of the greater Phoenix area and western Pinal counties. Damaging microburst winds and locally heavy rain accompanied the strong storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with strong gusty winds developed over the southeastern portion of the greater Phoenix area during the afternoon hours of August 18th. A trained weather spotter approximately 5 miles east of Sun Lakes estimated a peak wind gust to 60 mph. The strong winds were associated with a dust storm moving through the area; at the same time visibility had lowered to less than one quarter of a mile.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms developed across portions of south central Arizona, including the greater Phoenix area, during the late afternoon and evening hours on August 18th. The storms produced strong gusty outflow winds which caused a dust storm over the lower deserts, including portions of the greater Phoenix area and western Pinal counties. Damaging microburst winds and locally heavy rain accompanied the strong storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms developed over the south central deserts during the late afternoon hours on August 18th. The strong outflow winds from the storms generated a dust storm which moved steadily from the southeastern valley towards the northwest from approximately 5 pm through 8 pm. Much of the greater Phoenix area was affected. A number of trained weather spotters reported visiblities down to one quarter of a mile or below in dense blowing dust. A weather spotter 5 miles east of Sun Lakes reported visibility below one quarter of a mile in blowing dust at 520 pm. At 523 pm the official weather station at Mesa Gateway airport measured a visibility of one quarter mile.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure moved across northern Arizona. Southwest winds on the south side of the low created high winds across southeast Arizona.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds reached up to 40 mph for several hours across southeast Arizona as a large low pressure system tracked through the region. Several wind gusts in excess of 58 mph were also observed. A few gusts even approached 70 mph. No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced heavy rain over the eastern rim country north of Payson.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The remote weather station on Promontory Point picked up 2.97 inches of rain in less than two hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced heavy rain in and around Heber in the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced 1.53 inches of rain in Heber-Overgaard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced heavy rain in and around Heber in the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced 1.43 inches of rain on the southeast side of Heber-Overgaard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced heavy rain in and around Heber in the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Over 2.50 inches of rain fell about 8 miles northwest of Heber-Overgaard. There was lots of water everywhere.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported a possible tornado near Snowflake.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Possible tornado sighted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced heavy rain and flooding across central Navajo County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding covered State Route 99 with at least two feet of water at mile marker 29.  The water covered the road (depth unknown) at mile marker 27.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced heavy rain and flooding across central Navajo County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced 2.25 inches of rain in an hour in Pinetop-Lakeside.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck and killed a man at a campground near Big Lake.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 46 year old man was stuck and killed by lightning at a National Forest Campground at a National Forest campground near Big Lake, Arizona.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from thunderstorms produced flash flooding near Greer and Alpine.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Apache Country Road 2153 (unmaintained) near Alpine flooded and was impassable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from thunderstorms produced flash flooding in and below the Schultz Fire area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain over the Schultz Fire area caused flash flooding in the community below the fire area.  Baseball to soft ball sized rocks were washed across some roads, a culvert was plugged with rocks/debris, some residents lost power, and the flooding cut new channels through previous alluvial fans.  County and city crews worked for over two hours to clean and open roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms developed across portions of south central Arizona, including the greater Phoenix area, during the late afternoon and evening hours on August 18th. The storms produced strong gusty outflow winds which caused a dust storm over the lower deserts, including portions of the greater Phoenix area and western Pinal counties. Damaging microburst winds and locally heavy rain accompanied the strong storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with locally heavy rainfall developed in the area around Magma during the late afternoon hours on August 18th. Rainfall, with rates estimated in excess of one inch per hour, led to flash flooding on the Hunt Highway west of Magma. The flooding was reported by an amateur radio operator.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered high based thunderstorms developed across southwestern Arizona, including the Yuma area, during the evening hours on August 27th. The storms generated gusty wind in excess of 58 mph which produced some damage across portions of Yuma.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered strong thunderstorms moved across portions of southwestern Arizona, including the community of Yuma, during the late evening hours on August 27th. The storms generated gusty and damaging winds, estimated to be in excess of 55 mph. A trained weather spotter one mile north of the Fortuna Foothills reported numerous awnings and shutters damaged or torn from their houses.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered high based thunderstorms developed across southwestern Arizona, including the Yuma area, during the evening hours on August 27th. The storms generated gusty wind in excess of 58 mph which produced some damage across portions of Yuma.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered high based thunderstorms developed over southwestern Maricopa county during the evening hours on August 27th. The storms generated strong and gusty outflow winds. The Oatman RAWS weather station measured a wind gust of 62 mph at 852 pm. The wind gust did not cause any damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe thunderstorm development had occured in the early afternoon hours of July 5th near the Tucson, AZ area and east of Tucson. As thunderstorms dissipated and began moving northwest, outflows from these storms began producing very strong downburst winds, some in excess of 70 mph. Around 6:30pm MST, the NWS Phoenix office began receiving calls of dust moving into the Casa Grande, AZ area, about 50 miles southeast of downtown Phoenix. Upon examining radar data, it is estimated the dust storm reached a maximum height of 5000 feet to 6000 feet, the aerial coverage was close to 100 miles and the distance traveled was around 150 miles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A massive haboob moved across the greater Phoenix area during the evening hours on July 5, and winds were reported to be in excess of 50 knots. A trained weather spotter reported that the strong winds associated with the dust storm caused a carport to collapse near the intersection of McClintock and Ray roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from thunderstorms produced flash flooding in and below the Schultz Fire area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain over the Schultz Fire area caused flash flooding in the community below the fire area.  Baseball to soft ball sized rocks were washed across some roads, a culvert was plugged with rocks/debris, some residents lost power, and the flooding cut new channels through previous alluvial fans.  County and city crews worked for over two hours to clean and open roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Willow Fire was human caused on June 19th approximately 13 miles northwest of Forest Lakes, AZ. The fire was turned back to the local district at 600 PM on June 23rd. Full containment was achieved by the end of June. Crews still patrolled the fire into mid July.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Willow Fire was human caused and burned about 213 acres with 292 people assigned to the fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold and wet Pacific low pressure system continued to move east into New Mexico during the morning hours on December 4th. Partial clearing combined with wet surface conditions allowed areas of dense fog to form over the south central deserts. Many locations across the greater Phoenix area reported visibilities at or below one quarter of a mile. Dense fog was also reported from Gila bend eastwards into the Casa Grande area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A cold and wet Pacific low pressure system moved to the east, and into New Mexico, during the morning hours on December 4th. Partial clearing combined with wet grounds allowed areas of dense fog to develop over portions of the south central Deserts. Some of the most dense fog occurred over the east and southeast portions of the greater Phoenix metropolitan area. Numerous trained weather spotters reported dense fog, with visibilities at or below one quarter of a mile. Locations experiencing dense fog included Sun Lakes, Chandler Heights, Gilbert, Paradise Valley and Mesa. A spotter at Ocotillo and Higley roads reported visibility down to 2 car lengths in dense fog at 748 am. Satellite and spotter reports also indicated that dense fog was present from the Gila Bend area east to Casa Grande.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front brought widespread gusty north winds.  The winds accelerated through the favorably oriented terrain of the Colorado River Valley, causing damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds blew down several trees and tree limbs in Bullhead City, AZ.  One tree took out a set of power lines, knocking out power to 3800 customers.  The Bullhead City airport recorded a gust to 52 mph during the event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold and slow moving Pacific low pressure system slowly moved east across Arizona on the weekend of December 3rd and 4th. The low brought significant winter weather to portions of central Arizona, including locally heavy snow over the higher terrain east of Phoenix and significant amounts of graupel and small hail over the higher desert elevations of south central Arizona. Snow levels fell to around 3500 feet early Sunday morning east of Phoenix.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A cold Pacific low pressure system moved slowly across Arizona on Saturday, December 4th, and brought wintery weather to the the lower deserts of south central Arizona. Scattered to numerous showers occurred ahead of the main low center, and the cold airmass resulted in significant frozen precipitation, including graupel, small hail and some snow. Numerous trained weather spotters across the greater Phoenix area reported pea sized hail during the afternoon hours. At approximately 750 pm a spotter located at 99th Avenue and Happy Valley road reported pea sized hail up to 2 inches deep. Graupel and small hail were reported at 15th Avenue and Osborne. In addition, at 930 pm a spotter near Lake Pleasant reported 1 to 3 inches of a snow/hail mix at Scorpion Bay. The frozen precipitation made for hazardous driving conditions across the Phoenix metropolitan area during the day on Saturday. Fortunately, no major accidents or injuries were reported due to the dangerous driving conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dry low pressure system generated strong gusty winds across portions of south central arizona during the evening hours on January 14 2011. Peak gusts in southern Gila county were in excess of 50 mph, sufficient to blow rocks onto area highways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained weather spotter near Globe reported strong and gusty non-thunderstorm winds late Friday evening. Sustained winds approaching 40 mph were reported along with a measured gust to 60 mph. The winds caused two half-inch thick plywood sheds to be blown down. In addition, the Gila County sheriff's dispatch indicated that a motorist on highway 77 15 miles south of Globe called in a report of very strong winds. The gusty winds caused rocks to be blown onto the highway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving Pacific storm produced isolated heavy snow in the higher terrain of Mohave County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The observer at Diamond M Ranch measured 6.5 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clearing skies and wet grounds in the wake of a departing Pacific low pressure system led to the development of dense fog across the western Arizona deserts during the morning hours on December 15th. Many locations along the lower Colorado river valley and across the southwest Arizona deserts observed dense fog which lowered visibilities to near zero miles, beginning during the early morning hours. By noon, the fog had mostly dissipated.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Clearing skies and wet ground in the wake of a departing Pacific low pressure system led to the development of widespread dense fog over the western Arizona deserts during the morning hours on December 14th. A number of trained weather spotters reported dense fog with visibility at or below one quarter mile. A spotter one mile west of Tacna reported dense fog with visibility down to 100 yards at 954 am. Dense fog was also reported by the Parker Sheriff's Office; they indicated that dense fog in Vicksburg Junction lowered visibility below 10 feet at 330 am. Fortunately, no significant accidents or injuries were reported due to the low visibilities and potentially dangerous driving conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The ground across southwest and south central Arizona remained moist on December 15th, and with light wind and generally clear skies, locally dense fog developed again over the lower Arizona desert during the morning hours. Although patchy dense fog occurred from Phoenix westward to the lower Colorado river, some of the most dense fog occurred over portions of the southeast greater Phoenix area. Several trained spotters reported visibility at or below one quarter mile during the morning hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally clear skies, light wind and lingering low level moisture allowed locally dense fog to form over portions of the lower central and southwestern Arizona desert during the morning hours on December 15th. At 725 am, a trained weather spotter reported dense fog with a visibility down to one quarter mile at the intersection of Gilbert Road and the Hunt Highway, about 5 miles east of Sun Lakes. Another weather spotter in Queen Creek near the San Tan Mountain reported visibility down to 100 feet at 810 am. The fog disrupted the morning rush hour across the greater Phoenix area, and hindered travel along other major Arizona roads including Interstate 10 and Highway 60. Fortunately no significant accidents or injuries were reported with the hazardous driving conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two cold and wet winter storms moved through Arizona during the period from Saturday December 3rd through Monday December 5th. An elderly couple, whose car broke down on a isolated road east of Globe on December 1st, waited for help to arrive but eventually became trapped by the cold wintery weather. After the second storm moved east of the area, the couple decided to seek help and they set out on foot. The combined stress of several days without food in very cold conditions caused the 82 year old woman to collapse and die shortly after leaving the car on Tuesday December 6th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An elderly couple, Mr and Mrs Davis, left Globe on Thursday December 1st with the intent to drive northeast on US 60 to Socorro, New Mexico. They accidentally missed their turn and headed east on Highway 70. After realizing the mistake they attempted to rejoin US 60 by taking a remote forest road that appeared to reconnect with the highway. The road, most likely Indian Road 10 out of San Carlos, was remote and rocky. About 10 miles north on this road, and in very isolated high country, the vehicle became disabled after the transmission hit some rocks and broke.  The couple attempted to wait in the car for help, and unfortunately a pair of cold and wet winter storms moved into southern Gila county and trapped them in their car. The storms brought locally heavy snow and very cold temperatures. Mr and Mrs Davis survived in the vehicle for 5 days with no food and water. While gas remained in the car, they periodically turned it off and on to generate heat. The vehicle ran out of gas on Tuesday December 6th and since the storms had passed the couple decided to try and reach the highway on foot. Detectives indicated that Elizabeth Davis only made it a few hundred feet before she collapsed and died. Mr Davis tried to revive his wife, then continued walking. He was eventually found by an officer from the Arizona Game and Fish Department near US 60 just before noon on December 6th. He was dehydrated, disoriented, but alive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure center moving across the Great Basin produced locally strong winds.  The strongest winds were on the lee side of large terrain features including the Kaibab Plateau and the San Francisco Peaks.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts to 60 MPH were measured in Doney Park down wind from the San Francisco Peaks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific low pressure system and associated cold front moved east across southern Arizona during the afternoon and evening hours on November 4th. The front moved into the greater Phoenix area during the late evening hours, and produced breezy to windy conditions. Even though the atmosphere was relatively stable,  a few weak thunderstorms were able to form in the vicinity of the front. A couple of these storms generated strong winds in excess of 60 mph, which prompted the issuance of severe thunderstorm warnings. In addition, the winds were sufficient to blow down a large tree in the Gilbert area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved east across the greater Phoenix area during the late evening hours on November 4th. Isolated weak thunderstorms developed in the vicinity of the cold front. The combination of the front and the thunderstorm downdrafts produced gusty and damaging winds. A trained weather spottered reported that strong winds blew down a tree 1/8th of a mile west of the intersection of Germann and Power roads in Gilbert. The spotter estimated the tree height at 20 feet, and the diameter at 1 foot. Peak wind gusts were estimated to be around 60 knots.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm moved eastward across Arizona during the afternoon and evening hours on December 24, and it brought showers of sleet and snow to the higher terrain of southern Gila county. Winter weather advisories were issued, and the frozen precipitation led to dangerous driving conditions northeast of Superior. Several accidents forced the closure of area highways, including highway 60 northeast of Superior.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A cold and wet Pacific low pressure system moved across Arizona on Thursday, December 22nd and it brought winter weather conditions to the higher terrain of southern Gila county. A winter weather advisory was issued for the evening hours, and was cancelled shortly after midnight. Snow and sleet were reported in the area around Roosevelt between 530 and 615 pm. By 730 pm, one half inch of new snow was reported in the Globe area by a trained weather spotter. The snow and sleet led to slippery and dangerous driving conditions; at 730 pm the Globe Sheriff's office reported that highway 60 was closed north of Globe at MP 255 due to snow accumulation. At 810 pm, they also reported multiple 911 calls about accidents on US 60 near Top-of-the-World near MP 238. DPS closed the highway in that area due to dangerous driving conditions. Fortunately, no serious injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific low pressure system and associated cold front moved east across southern Arizona during the afternoon and evening hours on November 4th. The front moved into the greater Phoenix area during the late evening hours, and produced breezy to windy conditions. Even though the atmosphere was relatively stable,  a few weak thunderstorms were able to form in the vicinity of the front. A couple of these storms generated strong winds in excess of 60 mph, which prompted the issuance of severe thunderstorm warnings. In addition, the winds were sufficient to blow down a large tree in the Gilbert area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across the greater Phoenix metropolitan area during the late evening hours on November 4th. Isolated weak thunderstorms developed in association with the cold front. The combination of the frontal passage and thunderstorm downdrafts produced a measured wind gust of 61 mph at Phoenix Sky Harbor Airport. The wind gust was recorded by the official ASOS wind sensor. The time of the gust was 1111 pm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific low pressure system and associated cold front moved slowly east across the Arizona deserts during the day on November 4th.  During the afternoon hours, strong southerly winds developed across the south-central Arizona deserts, ahead of the approaching cold front. Due to the existing very dry conditions, the winds were able to generate dense blowing dust which persisted for many hours. Dust storm conditions were seen periodically, mainly to the south and east of the greater Phoenix metropolitan area. Visibilities fell to zero miles near Casa Grande during the early afternoon, and forced the closure of Interstate 8 at Bianco Road.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A cold front slowly moved east across the southern Arizona deserts during the day on November 4th. Strong southerly winds developed ahead of the front, and due to very dry conditions, they created periods of dense blowing dust. At times, dust storm conditions developed, mainly south and east of the greater Phoenix area. At 212 pm, the Department of Highways reported that visibilities as low as zero miles forced the closure of Interstate 8 at Bianco road, approximately 7 miles southwest of Casa Grande. Earlier at 140 pm, a trained weather spotter in Sacaton reported 1/4 mile visibility in a dust storm. Later, at 3 pm, another trained spotter 5 miles of Sun Lakes reported 1/8 mile visibility in a dust storm, with winds gusting to 35 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure center moving across the Great Basin produced locally strong winds.  The strongest winds were on the lee side of large terrain features including the Kaibab Plateau and the San Francisco Peaks.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The RAWS at House Rock reported wind gusts above 58 MPH for about 8 hours. The highest wind gust was 77 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually cold air mass had a significant impact on Southeast Arizona.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures dropped into the mid to upper teens across the Tucson area overnight resulting in numerous burst water pipes. A water pipe at a main Metro Water location froze, leaving almost 30 residences and businesses without water on the northwest side. More than 200 customers in Tucson reported frozen or burst water pipes. In all, at least 2000 residents and businesses were without water at some point during the day. AAA saw a 20% increase in local calls on Thursday, mostly about dead car batteries. Davis-Monthan Air Force Base had several buildings damaged by flooding frozen fire sprinkler pipes, which resulted in the buildings being closed for safety reasons. The intersection of Grant Road and Stone Ave. was also closed due to a burst water main that was causing slick road conditions. The Reid Park Zoo was also closed due to numerous broken water pipes.  ||Southwest Gas Company also could not meet natural gas demand, which resulted in about 14,000 Tucson customers being without heat.||In Green Valley, over $17,000 in damage was sustained at 11 Green Valley recreational centers due to broken sprinkler systems. ||Untold numbers of plants, trees, and shrubs were also killed by the record cold, including the prized southern Arizona Saguaro Cactus.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually cold air mass had a significant impact on Southeast Arizona.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several communities across the Upper San Pedro River Valley suffered from frozen and burst water pipes as temperatures dropped into the single digits to low teens. In addition, 4500 Sierra Vista Southwest Gas customers lost their natural gas flow. Most of the damage was at Fort Huachuca where over 400 homes suffered from broken water pipes with more than 100 homes sustaining damage. About 40 outpost buildings had ceilings collapse due to broken water pipes. Sierra Vista Fire Department also responded to several fire calls related to space heaters and candles.||In Benson, the city received over 100 calls regarding burst water pipes. The San Pedro Terrace Apartments on Pearl Street and the Desert Road Inn suffered damage from burst pipes and had to be evacuated. Residents and guests were transported to Sierra Vista hotels. A water pipe burst in the Benson Middle School Library ceiling causing damage to the ceiling, carpets, and shelves. Books on the shelves sustained light damage. The Benson Visitors and Community Center, Benson Public Library, and Cochise College Benson Center also sustained damage.||The St. David School District reported damage to one boiler, the main fire raiser for the sprinkler system, broken pipes in the weight-room wall and one drinking fountain. School did not have to be canceled.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was an extended period with wind chill values at or below -20 degrees Fahrenheit across the higher elevations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful Pacific storm system produced high winds, including extreme downslope winds, and locally heavy high elevation snow over the Mojave Desert and southern Great Basin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This gust occurred at the Grand Canyon Skywalk (11 miles E Meadview).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful Pacific storm system produced high winds, including extreme downslope winds, and locally heavy high elevation snow over the Mojave Desert and southern Great Basin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Graupel and small hail fell on Highway 68, contributing to five accidents involving seven vehicles.  One person was killed (indirect fatality) and two were injured.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Much of the Mojave Desert experienced gusty winds behind a strong cold front. One home suffered minor damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds tore a section of foam and plywood roofing material from a home in Bullhead City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably cold storm system brought locally heavy snow to the higher elevations of Mohave County Arizona.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The observer at Diamond M Ranch (5492') measured a total of 14.2 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Gusty southeast winds created low visibilities for motorists along hwy 347 in Pinal County in the early hours on the morning of March 21, 2011. Later the same day, during the afternoon and evening hours, rain showers developed over Pinal county as well as northwest Maricopa and southern Gila county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pinal County Sheriff's Office Dispatch called to notify a visibility of 1/4 mile. Field officers also confirmed dense blowing dust along state highway 347.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced heavy rain in Forest Lakes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced 1.70 inches of rain in just 45 minutes. Minor street flooding was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced heavy rain in Tonto Village in northern Gila County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter in Tonto Village reported approximately 1.50 inches of rain in 30 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck Mohave Point on the South Rim of the Grand Canyon.  One park visitor was killed and five others were injured.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning killed a 64-year-old woman visiting the Grand Canyon National Park. It is unclear if the lightning directly hit the victim. Five additional park visitors, ages 45 to 68,  were also injured.  The event took place at Mohave Point.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms brought heavy rain to the Western Mogollon Rim and portions of northeast Arizona. Heavy rain caused flash flooding that closed at least one road near Kayenta.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding was reported across Arizona State Route 160 near mile post 365 about 25 miles southwest of Kayenta.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rain caused flash flooding in the Wallow Burn area near Nutrioso.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding flooding and debris flows were reported along side canyons and over road ways in the eastern side of Greer. Rocks one foot in diameter washed over roads. The creek in Butler Canyon over flowed its banks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rain caused flash flooding in the Wallow Burn area near Nutrioso.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding was reported in Nutrioso with sheet flow over fields on the east side of town. Nutrioso Creek was running bank full with black water (ash) and debris. Over an inch and a half (1.60 inches) of rain fell between 11 AM and Noon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced heavy rain over the Wallow Burn Area causing flash flooding and debris/mud flows.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A foot of water was running down Butler Canyon which is normally dry.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extremely cold Canadian airmass settled over the the Arizona desert during the first week of February 2011. The cold dry air set the stage for a hard freeze that lasted for two days. The subfreezing conditions caused pipes to burst and caused widespread damage to frost-sensitive plants and crops.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Yuma county's Cooperative Extension Director reported that most, if not all, of the growing areas in Yuma county were a affected by a hard freeze that occurred during the mornings of February 3rd and 4th.  According to Kurt Nolte, some fields of leafy green vegatables were wiped out by the freeze while others had significant damage. The official low temperatures at the Yuma weather station were 30 degrees on the 3rd and 32 on the 4th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong, wet Pacific storm system and its associated cold front moved east across south-central Arizona Saturday night and into the day on Sunday, February 27. Heavy snow fell across the higher terrain of southern Gila county, with measured snowfall of two inches or more down to elevations near 3000 feet. The storm also produced pea-sized hail and moderate rainfall over the lower deserts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A cold front passed through southern Gila county during the morning hours on Sunday February 27. Locally heavy snow fell down to elevations near 3000 feet, starting near sunrise and continuing into the early afternoon. Two inches or more of snow fell in the Globe-Miami area, at elevations near 3500 feet. This satisfied local criteria for heavy snow. Pea sized hail and sleet were also reported at lower desert elevations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front pushed east across south central Arizona from the late evening on the 26th into the early morning hours on February 27th. Strong and gusty winds with the frontal passage caused damage in portions of the greater Phoenix metropolitan area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front, associated with a cold and wet Pacific storm, pushed east across south central Arizona on Saturday night. The strongest winds, with gusts estimated to be near 50 mph, occurred from near midnight Saturday into the early morning hours on Sunday. The winds caused significant damage to the Great Fair, located on Avenue of the Fountains and Saguaro Boulevard. 35 vendor tents were either destroyed or significantly damaged. The damage to the fair was among the worst seen in the past 15 years; some vendors described the aftermathas if a wind burst or mini-tornado had swept through the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced heavy rain over the Wallow Burn Area causing flash flooding and debris/mud flows.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from a thunderstorm over the Wallow Burn area caused flash flooding,  debris flows, and mud flows across Highway 180 about a mile north of Nutrioso. There was a motorcycle accident due to the mud on the roadway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong and energetic Pacific storm system swept across Arizona with heavy rain and mountain snow. The snow level started off around 7000 feet with heavy wet snow with heavy rain below this elevation.  The snow level then dropped to around 4000 feet. The highest elevations reported over three feet of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ten inches of snow fell 3 miles southwest of Prescott.  The City of Prescott reported 4.5 inches of snow. The weather contributed to a three vehicle wreck on Interstate 40 just west of Ash Fork. Several cars slid off Interstate 40 between mile marker 100 westward to the Coconino County line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system brought strong winds to southern Arizona resulting in downed power lines, blowing dust, and reduced visibilities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter recorded a wind gust of 63 mph at 12:45 pm. The same spotter later recorded a gust of 73 mph at 119 pm. The high winds downed numerous telephone and cable television lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system resulted in strong winds across southern Arizona.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds threw an inflatable jumping castle into the air, later landing a 9 year old girl on a neighboring rooftop. The castle traveled about 100 feet and landed about 13 feet off the ground. The girl suffered serious head injuries. An 11-year old girl was also injured as she fell out of the castle as it flew through the air. It occurred in Continental Ranch in Marana.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system brought strong winds to southern Arizona resulting in downed power lines, blowing dust, and reduced visibilities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cochise County Sheriff's Office reported that a brush fire created dense smoke and the closure of Highway 90 between Camino de Tundra and Camino de Manana on Highway 90 in the Whetstone area.

CZ_NAME: UPPER GILA RIVER AND ARAVAIPA VALLEYS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually cold air mass had a significant impact on Southeast Arizona.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures dropped into the single digits across the Gila River Vally with wind chills dropping as low as -10 degrees. As a result, a water main line broke at Fifth Avenue and Second Street. More than 100 calls were received by Safford Utility in response to frozen pipes that later turned to broken pipes. Safford Fire Department responded to more than a dozen calls for malfunctioning sprinkler systems. Some businesses that received minor water damage were the Arizona Credit Union, the Veterans Administration Office and Thriftee Supermarket. ||In Thatcher, the Fire Department responded to the Thatcher school where sprinkler systems and backflow preventers in several buildings burst. ||Pima also had numerous water lines burst as a result of the cold. One pipe burst at the entrance to the Pima Fire Station. The Graham County Jail also had water damage sustained in the honor pod by a broken pipe.||Fire Departments in Safford, Thatcher and Pima also responded to fires involving space heaters and candles used to keep water pipes from freezing.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong and energetic Pacific storm system swept across Arizona with heavy rain and mountain snow. The snow level started off around 7000 feet with heavy wet snow with heavy rain below this elevation.  The snow level then dropped to around 4000 feet. The highest elevations reported over three feet of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy wet snow began to fall early Saturday morning February 19th.  By the time snow stopped falling Sunday afternoon between one and 3 and half feet of snow had piled up around the area. Some snowfall totals include(in inches) : Snowbowl 44, Flagstaff Airport 17.9, Bellemont (WFO) 15.3, 4 miles south of Williams 14.8,  Munds Park area 8 to 13, Kachina Village 12.4,  Parks 12, and Williams 11.  Northbound Interstate 17 was closed for a hour and a half south of Flagstaff. Highway 89 was closed between Sedona and Flagstaff and 12 mile stretch of Highway 180 north of Flagstaff was also closed due to snow and blowing snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually cold air mass had a significant impact on Southeast Arizona.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Record cold resulted in two fatalities in the Tucson Metro Area. A woman in her late 30's was found dead in an alley near East Speedway and North Campbell Ave. A second woman was also found dead near East Grant and North Craycroft. An autopsy was performed.  Another person was also found lying out in the cold nearly frozen and was taken to the hospital with non-life threatening injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually cold air mass had a significant impact on Southeast Arizona.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures dropped to as low as 0 degrees across eastern Cochise County as several communities experienced frozen or burst water pipes. Most notably, Willcox received over 790 calls for frozen or burst water pipes. Damage was also done to Safeway when a pipe burst in the ceiling causing several ceiling tiles to collapse. The Willcox School District had a broken pipe in the wood shop room and a back flow preventer burst outside the wood shop causing minor water damage.||In Douglas, several businesses were closed due to broken pipes including; Chiricahua Community Health Center, Copper Queen Medical Associates, the Douglas Library and APS Energy. Douglas School District also canceled classes due to burst pipes. The worst damage was done to a home on Fourth Street when the pipes in the attic burst causing the ceiling to collapse and the home to be flooded.

CZ_NAME: UPPER SANTA CRUZ RIVER AND ALTAR VALLEYS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually cold air mass had a significant impact on Southeast Arizona.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Upper Santa Cruz River Vally experienced numerous issues with broken water pipes as temperatures dipped into the upper single digits to mid teens. Robert Bracker Elementary School in Nogales was canceled due to broken pipes. An underground water line ruptured in the Monte Carlo neighborhood, where the road had to be dug up to replace the line. Also, an air release valve on a 16 inch pipeline that feeds the city water system from the Santa Cruz River burst, blasting water into the air. The Nogales Fire Department also responded to a two car collision caused by icy roads on Target Range Road. Four people were involved and two were injured and taken to the Holy Cross Hospital. ||In Patagonia, an elderly resident had to be evacuated to her sister's house after the pipes in the ceiling burst causing drywall to fall.||Also, ranchers near Sonoita reported a water pump bursting causing a cattle tank with thousands of gallons of water to drain out.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system brought strong winds to southern Arizona resulting in downed power lines, blowing dust, and reduced visibilities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds downed power lines east of Naco, sparking a brush fire that resulted in an hour-long closure of roads in the Bisbee Junction area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific storm brought widespread rain and snow to northern Arizona April 8 and 9th. Light rain and snow began to develop over the Mogollon Rim during the morning of April 8 and then heavier showers began to spread across western Arizona during the afternoon. The rain and snow above about 4500 feet became heavy by evening. The strong cold front slowly moved across the state allowing snow to pile up in the higher elevations. The snow level dropped to as low as 3500 feet once the front moved through. The storm pushed east of Arizona by morning of April 10 leaving patchy fog and very cold temperatures in its wake.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nine to Twelve inches of snow fell over the Kaibab Plateau.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoon moisture lingered over the Mojave Desert and southern Great Basin, continuing to fuel thunderstorms.  Some storms produced severe weather and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A citizen posted a report of penny size hail to WFO Las Vegas' Facebook page.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms developed across the central Arizona deserts during the early evening hours of July 4th, and they produced strong gusty and damaging microburst winds. The strong winds damaged trees over portions of Pinal county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms in the Casa Grande area produced damaging microburst winds during the early evening hours on July 4th. Law enforcement personnel reported that strong winds, estimated to be around 50 knots, downed a large tree. The falling tree landed on power lines, knocking them to the ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms developed over the south central deserts during the evening hours on the 9th of July. Gusty downburst winds up to 60 mph generated a dust storm that primarily affected the eastern portions of the greater Phoenix area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms over the south central deserts produced strong downburst winds during the evening hours on July 9th. The strong winds produced a dust storm that affected the eastern portions of the greater Phoenix area, including Mesa and Apache Junction. Shortly after 8 pm, a trained weather spotter reported winds gusting to 60 mph with visibility down to one quarter mile in blowing dust at the intersection of Loop 202 and Ellsworth Roads. Additionally, the strong winds blew the awning off of a mobile home.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms developed over the south central deserts during the evening hours on the 9th of July. Gusty downburst winds up to 60 mph generated a dust storm that primarily affected the eastern portions of the greater Phoenix area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with strong and gusty downburst winds developed over the south central deserts during the evening hours on July 9th. The winds, gusting as high as 60 mph, produced a significant dust storm which affected the eastern portions of the greater Phoenix area, including the communities of Mesa and Apache Junction. A trained weather spotter 5 miles east of Mesa reported 50 mph winds and visibility reduced below one quarter mile in blowing dust. Another spotter near Baseline Road and Sossaman also reported 50 mph wind gusts as well as one quarter mile visibility in blowing dust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated high based thunderstorms developed across La Paz county during the late afternoon and evening hours. The storms produced damaging microburst winds which toppled trees, damaged homes and flattened carports. No injuries were reported with these thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Isolated high based thunderstorms developed over La Paz county during the late afternoon and evening hours on July 10th. The storms generated gusty microburst winds, estimated to be in excess of 60 knots. According to Cate's Column in the Parker Live Online paper, significant damage occurred in the community of Bouse. Strong winds lifted the roof off of a house and tossed it down a hill. The winds smashed windows, blew out metal garage doors, plucked coolers from rooftops, flattened several carports, and uprooted a number of Mesquite and Palo Verde trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated high based thunderstorms developed across La Paz county during the late afternoon and evening hours. The storms produced damaging microburst winds which toppled trees, damaged homes and flattened carports. No injuries were reported with these thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Isolated high based thunderstorms developed across La Paz county during the evening hours on July 10th. The storms produced wind gusts estimated to be in excess of 50 knots. Video footage taken by an amateur photographer showed several power poles being snapped by high winds along highway 95 north of Quartzsite. Video footage indicated that the poles were snapped between mileposts 112 and 113 on highway 95. Additional public reports indicated a total of 17 power poles were blown over or snapped by high winds.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began to fall over the Grand Canyon Country around daybreak on April 8th. Much heavier snow fell during the afternoon and evening.  Twelve inches fell at the South Rim and 9 inches fell over the North Rim.

Northbound Interstate 17 was closed about 40 miles south of Flagstaff.  There was a two fatality crash on Interstate 40 about 15 miles east of Flagstaff.  Police and the Department of Public Safety reported numerous other slid offs and collisions around the Flagstaff area. Most were non-injury.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A short lived monsoon thunderstorm generated gusty microburst winds at Phoenix Sky Harbor airport during the afternoon of July 10, 2011. No damage was reported at the airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Long term drought continued into March resulting in an upgrade to D3 conditions across southeast Arizona.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Abnormally dry conditions continued during the month of March. The southeast quarter of the state received less than 25% of average rainfall year-to-date and less than 50% for the water year (October to September). This lack of rainfall resulted in an upgrade from D2 conditions to D3 conditions across much of Cochise County on March 8. Later in the month on March 29, all of Cochise County as well as Santa Cruz County, southern Graham County, southern Greenlee County, and eastern Pima County were upgraded to D3 conditions. Prolonged drought conditions helped worsen critical fire weather conditions which helped contribute to numerous small brush fires. The Arizona Drought Watch report for extreme southeast Cochise County, including the Rio Bavispe watershed, found many ponds that normally have water during March were dry. Also, intermittent streams that normally flow were dry in many or all segments. Little to no green forage existed in the grasses or forbs, and pipelines and troughs have replaced most pond water resulting in significant agriculture impacts. The Arizona Drought Watch report for extreme southeast Pima County, including the Santa Cruz River watershed, found that drought impacts are present and severe. The flow in the Santa Cruz is less than last year and about half the average flow seen within drought years. Rising severity was also observed. Water levels were approximately two feet lower than last year and approxiamately six feet lower than pre-drought levels.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong high pressure and record high temperatures created a very unstable and dry environment resulting in several dust devils.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong daytime heating resulting in record high temperatures and dry conditions created a ripe environment for dust devils. One dust devil formed near Speedway Blvd. and N. 11th Ave. where an annual Children's Festival was in progress. The dust devil quickly moved across the yard lifting a jumping castle several feet into the air with two children inside. The jumping castle then traveled several feet before getting caught in telephone wires and landing in the median of Speedway Blvd. A 9-year-old girl was serious injured in the accident, suffering a broken shoulder, leg, and several fractures to her face. A 10-year-old boy also sustained injuries to the back of his head.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extremely cold Canadian airmass settled over the the Arizona desert during the first week of February 2011. The cold dry air set the stage for a hard freeze that lasted for two days. The subfreezing conditions caused pipes to burst and caused widespread damage to frost-sensitive plants and crops.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A hard freeze occurred during the morning hours on February 3rd and 4th; many central desert locations experienced several hours or more of temperatures below 28 degrees. Morning low temperatures dropped into the upper teens in the coldest locations. Freezing conditions also occurred Wednesday morning February 2nd. Widespread damage to frost sensitive plants, such as ficus, occurred across the central deserts; many homes and businesses experienced frozen and burst pipes and well as furnace failures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure area slowly moving through the  Great Basin and Colorado brought snow to northern Arizona. Over a foot of snow fell over portions of the Mogollon Rim where the upslope flow enhanced the snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell over the Kaibab Plateau. Jacob Lake reported around 12 inches of snow and the North Rim of the Grand Canyon reported 14 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure area slowly moving through the  Great Basin and Colorado brought snow to northern Arizona. Over a foot of snow fell over portions of the Mogollon Rim where the upslope flow enhanced the snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began to fall by mid morning and increased in the afternoon.  Interstate 40 was closed in both directions for the afternoon Thursday as Arizona Department of Transportation worked with officers from the Arizona Department of Public Safety to remove the debris from a number of crashes in the eastbound lanes.  There were five different collisions with the largest involving nine vehicles. The was a 16 mile backup. One driver reported being delayed 11 hours. West bound lanes became blocked when vehicles could not make it up the hill just west of the I-17 interchange.  Eight people were transported to the Flagstaff Medical Center.  North bound I-17 was also blocked for a time due to collisions blocking the roadway.  Highway 180 north of Flagstaff was blocked for a time because of a jack-knifed semi.  Some snowfall totals include (inches): Bellemont 10.5, Flagstaff Airport 6.5, Mormon Lake 5, Munds Park 4, and 4 miles south of Williams 4.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extremely cold Canadian airmass settled over the the Arizona desert during the first week of February 2011. The cold dry air set the stage for a hard freeze that lasted for two days. The subfreezing conditions caused pipes to burst and caused widespread damage to frost-sensitive plants and crops.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A hard freeze occurred during the mornings of February 3rd and 4th; many locations across the greater Phoenix area experienced several hours at or below 28 degrees. Phoenix Sky Harbor reported low temperatures of 30 degrees on both mornings, and these lows were near record values for the dates. Low temperatures had not fallen to these levels since the last hard freeze in January 2007. Freezing temperatures also occurred the morning of February 2nd. Frozen greens caused cancellation of the Phoenix Waste Management Pro-Am on February 2nd, in addition the cold temperatures and frozen greens severly disrupted Open play all week and forced the tournament to finish on Monday. All of this led to significant decreases in attendance and loss of revenue for the Open. The hard freeze led to widespread damage to frost-sensitive plants, caused numerous water pipes to burst in the colder outlying areas, and caused damage to local crops such as peach and citrus. George Brazil reported dozens of calls from around the valley due to broken pipes and failed furnaces.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An estimated 15 inches of snow fell at Hualapai Mountain Lodge.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flagstaff Airport received 17.6 inches of new snow, Kachina Village17.0, Bellemont 13.5,  13.0 inches in northeast Flagstaff, Munds Park 13, 11 inches south of Williams,  and 9 inches in Parks.

CZ_NAME: LITTLE COLORADO RIVER VALLEY IN NAVAJO COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic front left very cold temperatures across northern Arizona to start out 2011. The cold temperatures froze pipes and caused flooding even in the usually coldest locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cold temperatures froze and broke a water valve in the Navajo County Complex.  Employees were sent home early as water flooded the areas around the buildings. Two water towers released approximately 400,000 gallons of water after the shut off valve broke.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic front left very cold temperatures across northern Arizona to start out 2011. The cold temperatures froze pipes and caused flooding even in the usually coldest locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Clear skies, no surface winds, fresh snow, and an arctic air mass allowed the temperature to plummet on New Years Morning.  The temperature at the Flagstaff Airport dropped to -19F and down to -30F at the WFO in Bellemont. Frozen pipes and flooding impacted many buildings across the Western Mogollon Rim. Seven buildings on the Northern Arizona University campus, two Flagstaff Unified School District buildings, a crafts store, and 40 to 60 more homes and apartments were affected by burst pipes.  The two boilers and a generator were destroyed ($450,000 replacement cost) at Sinagua Middle School when pipes burst and flooded the boiler room with 5 feet of water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic front left very cold temperatures across northern Arizona to start out 2011. The cold temperatures froze pipes and caused flooding even in the usually coldest locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Payson Roundup newspaper reported at least 48 cases of frozen water pipes in and around the Payson area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic front left very cold temperatures across northern Arizona to start out 2011. The cold temperatures froze pipes and caused flooding even in the usually coldest locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very cold temperatures caused water pipes to burnt in the South County Health Complex in Show Low.  The flooding that resulted caused the clouser of the complex for at least a week. The new buildings were still under warranty.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic front left very cold temperatures across northern Arizona to start out 2011. The cold temperatures froze pipes and caused flooding even in the usually coldest locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very cold temperatures in the Prescott area caused pipes to freeze and burst in over 120 buildings. Local plumbing and restoration businesses received over 400 call just as the New Year started.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very cold and windy arctic air mass moved into northern Arizona. This caused temperatures to plummet to near the coldest readings of all time. Strong northeasterly winds brought even colder wind chill temperatures. These cold temperatures, winds, and little snow cover on roofs brought another round of frozen pipes, busted pipes, and flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The temperatures stayed well below freezing for over 36 hours with strong northeasterly winds.  The Flagstaff airport experienced the 7th coldest recorded high temperature (12F) and had a windchill of -23F.  The sensor at top of Arizona Snowbowl (11,500 feet) had a wind chill of -64F.  The Flagstaff Fire Department received over two dozen calls on the 3rd for frozen pipes, busted pipes, and flooding.  Several schools were closed and several buildings on the Northern Arizona University campus were closed when sprinkler pipes froze and broke.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very cold and windy arctic air mass moved into northern Arizona. This caused temperatures to plummet to near the coldest readings of all time. Strong northeasterly winds brought even colder wind chill temperatures. These cold temperatures, winds, and little snow cover on roofs brought another round of frozen pipes, busted pipes, and flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very cold conditions lead to freezing and burst pipes in the White Mountains.  About two dozen businesses,  schools, and homes had frozen pipes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure area slowly moving through the  Great Basin and Colorado brought snow to northern Arizona. Over a foot of snow fell over portions of the Mogollon Rim where the upslope flow enhanced the snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell over the Eastern Mogollon Rim.  Snowfall totals include (inches): Linden 15.5 and Heber-Overgaard 10.5.

CZ_NAME: LITTLE COLORADO RIVER VALLEY IN NAVAJO COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous cold front moved across Arizona with rain, snow, and strong to very strong winds.  The winds caused areas of blowing dust and reduced visibility in the Little Colorado River Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gust exceeded 58 MPH for about 20 minutes at the Winslow Airport. Wind gusts exceeded 40 MPH for over 5 hours.  Blowing dust reduced the visibility below 2 miles during the strongest winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous cold front moved across Arizona with rain, snow, and strong to very strong winds.  The winds caused areas of blowing dust and reduced visibility in the Little Colorado River Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong and damaging winds were felt across Navajo County as a strong cold front moved from west t east across the area. Blowing dust also caused travel difficulties. Some of the strongest wind gusts reported at Window Rock was 60 MPH.

CZ_NAME: LITTLE COLORADO RIVER VALLEY IN COCONINO COUNTY
