EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gust exceeded 58 MPH at the ADOT anemometer near Two Guns along I-40 for about half an hour.  Wind gusts of over 40 MPH lasted for about 4 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold Pacific low pressure system brought rain throughout most of the day to the Greater Phoenix area as well as higher terrain areas of southern Gila county on April 9, 2011. Along with the rain showers, very cold air made its way into the valleys helping to set a record low maximum daily temperature at Phoenix Sky Harbor Airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: With the strong low pressure system over the region, the temperature at Phoenix Sky Harbor Airport reached a low of 45 degrees and only reached a high of 54 degrees, setting a daily record for a low maximum temperature. Numerous spotters reported pea size hail near the city of Glendale in the late afternoon, among other reports of rain changing to snow but melting as it fell to the ground near Globe and northern Scottsdale.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long term drought continued across southeast Arizona during the month of May.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extremely dry conditions continued during the month of May across all of southeast Arizona. Extreme drought conditions (D3) expanded to include all of Greenlee and Graham Counties, as well as extreme southeast Pinal County to start the month after an extremely dry April. May brought no measurable rainfall to a majority of southeast Arizona, including the Tucson Metro area. The White Mountains of northern Graham and Greenlee Counties received only a few hundredths of an inch of rain. The combination of extreme drought conditions, strong winds, hard winter freezes and the resulting dry fuels created extreme fire danger. This fire danger was realized on May 8 when a human caused fire in the Chiricahua Mountains, named Horseshoe 2, quickly grew out of control. Strong winds and prime fuels resulted in the Horseshoe 2 Fire growing over 9,000 acres on the first day alone. The Horseshoe 2 Fire charred 77,933 acres by the end of May. Over 83,000 acres had burned by the end of May in southeast Arizona with little containment. Arizona Drought Watch reported very low soil moisture resulting in plant stress in the Santa Cruz River Basin. Native low water use plants were severely drought stressed with some cacti dying.

A SUV lost control on the snow and collided with a school bus that was carrying children. Seven inches of snow fell about 5 miles south of Williams at an elevation of 7200 feet.  Two to three inches of snow fell in and around Flagstaff.

CZ_NAME: WHITE MOUNTAINS OF GRAHAM AND GREENLEE COUNTIES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The largest wildfire in Arizona state history started as an unattended campfire on May 29, 2011. Extreme drought conditions and strong winds allowed for this fire to quickly become out of control. By the time the fire was 100% contained during the beginning of July, 538,049 acres had been charred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong gusty winds, along with a continued prolonged drought allowed for an unattended campfire to quickly spread out of control. Thanks to strong winds, the fire grew 1,445 acres by the end of the second day. On the 31st, the fire grew another 1,171 acres for a total of 2,616 acres chard in May. Fire activity was located in extreme northern Greenlee County west of Highway 191 along County Road 25. No evacuation orders were issued by the end of May, although portions of the Apache-Sitgreaves National Forest were closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe to extreme drought conditions continued through the month of April across southeast Arizona.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dry conditions continued during the month of April across much of Arizona as only one significant winter storm moved through the state. Portions of southeast Cochise County received no measurable rainfall. Portions of south central Arizona received amounts from one tenth to one half inch while other areas, including eastern mountain ranges, received one tenth of an inch or less. More than 27,000 acres in Arizona have been consumed by wildfires through the end of April, mostly in the southern part of the state. Little change in the D3 condition area with all of Cochise and Santa Cruz Counties under D3 conditions. Portions of southern Greenlee, southern Graham, and eastern Pima were also still under D3 conditions. ||Arizona Drought Watch reported several impacts during the month. The Santa Cruz River basin of eastern Pima county reported unusually low flows in streams, rivers, and springs. Low soil moisture was resulting in plant stress, newly observed plant mortality, and oak leaves browning and falling with only 25% of Oaks flowering. Other unusual plant phenology was also observed with pines not producing cones and most flowers not budding at all. Dead oaks and ponderosas were quickly rotting at the base and falling within several months.||In the San Pedro River Basin in northern Cochise and southern Graham Counties, Arizona Drought Watch reported plant stress due to unusually low soil moisture. Native plants, including cacti and cotton plants are stressed, or have died.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably strong low pressure system moved across the Great Basin and produced high wind and dust over northern Arizona.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter just east of Chinle estimated wind gusts to be 65 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extreme drought conditions continued through May allowing for a human caused fire to quickly become out of control. Aided by strong southwest winds, a killer winter freeze, and extremely dry fuels, the fire ended up consuming over 200,000 acres of the Chiricahua Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Horseshoe 2 fire started on May 8 in Horseshoe Canyon in the Coronado National Forest. Gusty southwest winds caused the fire to grow over 9,000 acres on the first day. This extreme fire behavior was caused by extremely dry fuels caused by a long term drought and a killer winter freeze of oak trees. Residents of the communities of Paradise and Portal were evacuated at times due to thick smoke and from the threat of this rapidly growing wildfire overtaking these locations. By the end of May, 77,933 acres had been burned and 12 firefighters had suffered minor injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system approaching Arizona brought very windy conditions to much of northern Arizona.  The highest winds were recorded east of the San Fransisco Peaks near Flagstaff.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds were felt in Doney Park east of the San Fransisco Peaks along Highway 89.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong high pressure and dry conditions allowed daytime temperatures in Tucson to reach above 110 degrees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Well above normal temperatures were recorded in the Tucson area during the afternoon of July 2 with a high temperature reading of 111 degrees. This tied the record high for the day. As a result, a teenage boy was treated for dehydration while hiking during the late morning. Other locations across the western deserts of southern Arizona also reached heat advisory thresholds with daytime high temperatures up to 112 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long term drought continued across southeast Arizona during the month of May.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extremely dry conditions continued during the month of May across all of southeast Arizona. Extreme drought conditions (D3) expanded to include all of Greenlee and Graham Counties, as well as extreme southeast Pinal County to start the month after an extremely dry April. May brought no measurable rainfall to a majority of southeast Arizona, including the Tucson Metro area. The White Mountains of northern Graham and Greenlee Counties received only a few hundredths of an inch of rain. The combination of extreme drought conditions, strong winds, hard winter freezes and the resulting dry fuels created extreme fire danger. This fire danger was realized on May 8 when a human caused fire in the Chiricahua Mountains, named Horseshoe 2, quickly grew out of control. Strong winds and prime fuels resulted in the Horseshoe 2 Fire growing over 9,000 acres on the first day alone. The Horseshoe 2 Fire charred 77,933 acres by the end of May. Over 83,000 acres had burned by the end of May in southeast Arizona with little containment.  ||Arizona Drought Watch reported very low soil moisture resulting in plant stress in the Santa Cruz River Basin. Native low water use plants were severely drought stressed with some cacti dying.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved through the Great Basin region resulting in strong winds across southern Arizona.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong surface winds occurred in Sierra Vista. Homeland Security's aerostat, an anti-trafficking balloon, broke free from its tether and crashed into the Canyon de Flores neighborhood in Sierra Vista. Equipment from the aerostat was picked up from 39 different sites throughout the neighborhood. Minor damage was reported to a few vehicles and homes, as well as a damage to power lines south of Fort Huachuca. The aerostat, based at the fort since 1986, was a total loss and will be replaced.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong daytime heating and an unstable atmosphere created prime conditions for dust devils.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong daytime heating and unstable conditions caused several dust devils to form across the region. One dust devil formed at Hoffman Park located at the 4800 block of South Cecilia St. in Tucson. Parents were able to pull their children out of a jumping castle just before the stakes were pulled out of the ground as the jumping castle was lifted several feet into the funnel and tossed into a light pole. The dust devil also picked up several other pieces of debris before dissipating over the concrete street. Six children were injured by flying debris. Five were evaluated at the scene, while one 16-year-old boy was transported to a hospital with minor injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe to extreme drought conditions continued through the month of April across southeast Arizona.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dry conditions continued during the month of April across much of Arizona as only one significant winter storm moved through the state. Portions of southeast Cochise County received no measurable rainfall. Portions of south central Arizona received amounts from one tenth to one half inch while other areas, including eastern mountain ranges, received one tenth of an inch or less. More than 27,000 acres in Arizona have been consumed by wildfires through the end of April, mostly in the southern part of the state. Little change in the D3 condition area with all of Cochise and Santa Cruz Counties under D3 conditions. Portions of southern Greenlee, southern Graham, and eastern Pima were also still under D3 conditions. ||Arizona Drought Watch reported several impacts during the month. The Santa Cruz River basin of eastern Pima county reported unusually low flows in streams, rivers, and springs. Low soil moisture was resulting in plant stress, newly observed plant mortality, and oak leaves browning and falling with only 25% of Oaks flowering. Other unusual plant phenology was also observed with pines not producing cones and most flowers not budding at all. Dead oaks and ponderosas were quickly rotting at the base and falling within several months.||In the San Pedro River Basin in northern Cochise and southern Graham Counties, Arizona Drought Watch reported plant stress due to unusually low soil moisture. Native plants, including cacti and cotton plants are stressed, or have died.

CZ_NAME: UPPER SANTA CRUZ RIVER AND ALTAR VALLEYS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extreme drought conditions and strong winds caused a human-started fire to quickly become out of control. A separate fire, the Pajarita Fire, started in early June and later combined with the Murphy Fire. The two fires were known as the Murphy Complex Wildfire which persisted into mid June and consumed over 68,000 acres.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme drought conditions coupled with strong winds resulted in a human caused fire getting out of control and spreading into rugged terrain of Santa Cruz county. A total of 4,052 acres of grass, shrub, oak, and mesquite burned during May.

CZ_NAME: LITTLE COLORADO RIVER VALLEY IN APACHE COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous cold front moved across Arizona with rain, snow, and strong to very strong winds.  The winds caused areas of blowing dust and reduced visibility in the Little Colorado River Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong and damaging winds were felt across Navajo County as a strong cold front moved from west t east across the area. Blowing dust also caused travel difficulties. Some of the strongest wind gusts reported were Petrified Forest National Park 66 MPH and Lupton 62 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous cold front moved across Arizona with rain, snow, and strong to very strong winds.  The winds caused areas of blowing dust and reduced visibility in the Little Colorado River Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow falling across the Western Mogollon Rim made travel on area roads hazardous.  Interstate 17 was closed from about 6 miles south of the Coconino/Yavapai to Flagstaff due to a multiple car pile up and Highway 89 was closed from Sedona to Flagstaff due to a snow plow crash. An SUV rolled about 6 miles west of Williams and a passenger died in the crash.  The Department of Public Safety responded to over 18 crashes and 20 slide offs.  Ten of the crashes involved injuries. The Flagstaff Airport received 7.4 inches of snow, in the town Flagstaff 8 to 9 inches of snow, 4 miles west-northwest of Flagstaff 12 niches, Kachina Village/Mountainaire 7.0 to 9.4 inches, 3 miles north of Parks 6.0 inches, Bellemont 6.9 inches, southwest side of Williams 10 inches, and Snowbowl 5 (at 9500 feet) to 10 inches (at 10,800 feet).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions across southern Arizona eased somewhat toward the end of December.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drastic improvements were made to drought conditions during the first half of December as widespread beneficial rain fell across the entire region. The early December rainfall coupled with above normal rainfall in November was sufficient to remove D3 (extreme) and D4 (exceptional) drought conditions from all of southern Arizona by December 20th for the first time since March.  While rainfall will be beneficial to winter annuals and crops, little improvement was seen in streams, ponds, and stock tanks. The last half of the month ended with no additional rainfall, so the concern for a dry winter remains.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The second in a series of cold Pacific low pressure systems moved in from the northwest on Monday December 5th, and brought locally heavy snowfall to portions of southern Gila county. The system lowered the snow level down to near 2000 feet, and caused some snow to accumulate across the lower south central deserts, mainly the foothills to the north and east of greater Phoenix.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The second in a series of cold Pacific low pressure systems moved into central Arizona from the northwest on Monday December 5th. The system prompted the issuance of a winter storm warning above 3500 feet for southern Gila county during the daytime hours on Monday. According to a report from a Co-op observer, 4 inches of snow was observed in Globe as of 10 am. The snow level over south central Arizona lowered to near 2000 feet during the day, and light accumulations were observed across the foothills of the lower deserts, mainly north and east of greater Phoenix, in locations such as north Scottsdale, Carefree and Cave Creek. The Old Real Market building in Miami suffered roof damage caused by heavy snow, as awnings at the top of the building broke off and fell to the ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms developed over the central Arizona deserts during the late afternoon and evening hours on July 31st. Initially the storms produced a dust storm that moved from northwest Pinal county into the Phoenix metropolitan area. The dust storm was followed by additional thunderstorms that brought damaging microburst winds and heavy rains to the central deserts including the greater Phoenix area. Damaging microburst winds also affected the area around Casa Grande.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trained spotters in the greater Phoenix metropolitan area reported a dust storm during the evening hours on July 31st. The dust storm was spawned by thunderstorm outflow winds in excess of 40 mph. A trained spotter 5 miles east of Sun Lakes reported visibility less than one quarter mile in dense blowing dust at Gilbert and Riggs road at 640 pm. Winds were gusting to near 50 mph at the time. At approximately 730 pm, spotters near Firebird lake and at I-10 and Warner road reported a dust storm with visibilties below one quarter of a mile.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wet and slow moving Pacific low pressure system moved into Arizona during the evening hours on December 12th, and brought widespread showers to the central deserts which continued into the morning hours on December 14th. Locally heavy rainfall occurred across the central deserts; many locations recored more than one inch of rain with this event. The rainfall was sufficient to cause some road damage over portions of Pinal county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wet and slow moving Pacific low pressure system brought widespread showers, some with locally heavy rain, to the central Arizona deserts on December 13th. Rainfall continued through the morning hours on December 14th, and many lower desert locations recorded more than one inch of rainfall during the event. Local media reported that Hunt Highway, or State Route 87, was closed between Arizona Farms Road and Merrill Ranch Parkway due to surface damage to the road. The road was reported closed at 730 am on December 14th. The damage was due to the excessive rainfall that fell in the area. The location of the closure was south of the San Tan Valley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed across the south central Arizona deserts during the afternoon and evening hours on September 9th. The storms produced damaging outflow winds, estimated to be in excess of 50 knots. Several mobile homes were damaged, and power lines were downed in Pinal county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed over the south central Arizona deserts during the evening hours on September 9th. The storms produced strong, damaging winds estimated to be at least 50 knots. According to local law enforcement, strong winds caused a number of power lines to be downed near the town of Maricopa at approximately 8 pm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed across the south central Arizona deserts during the afternoon and evening hours on September 9th. The storms produced damaging outflow winds, estimated to be in excess of 50 knots. Several mobile homes were damaged, and power lines were downed in Pinal county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms with strong, damaging winds developed over the south central Arizona deserts during the evening hours on September 9th. A trained weather spotter reported that shingles were torn off the roofs of several homes located about 11 miles west of Stanfield. In addition, mobile home skirting was damaged or destroyed on several of the mobile homes. A wind gust to 49 mph was measured, however peak gusts were likely in excess of 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low along the southern California coast spread moisture into south central Arizona on the 10th. This led to scattered afternoon and evening thunderstorms, with the strongest storms located to the east of Central Phoenix. The storms produced gusty damaging winds in excess of 60 mph. Large hail and dense blowing dust also occurred across the lower south central deserts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms developed across the eastern portion of the greater Phoenix area during the evening hours on September 10th. At approximately 6 pm, a National Weather Service employee in east Mesa estimated a thunderstorm wind gust to 70 mph. No damage was reported with this gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low along the southern California coast spread moisture into south central Arizona on the 10th. This led to scattered afternoon and evening thunderstorms, with the strongest storms located to the east of Central Phoenix. The storms produced gusty damaging winds in excess of 60 mph. Large hail and dense blowing dust also occurred across the lower south central deserts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms with damaging microburst winds affected part of the eastern Phoenix metropolitan area during the evening hours on September 10th. A trained weather spotter, near Brown and Ellsworth roads in east Mesa, reported that trees were downed due to winds estimated at 70 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong, deep, and cold low pressure center moved down the West Coast and then across Arizona.  This storm brought heavy snow to much of the area even at lower elevations. The very cold nature of this storm maximized precipitation efficiency at lower elevations while some upper elevations received less snow. This storm also brought strong winds over and northeast of the Mogollon Rim.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Twelve inches of snow fell in Clints Well and 10 inches of snow fell in Forest Lakes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold and slow moving Pacific low pressure system slowly moved east across Arizona on the weekend of December 3rd and 4th. The low brought significant winter weather to portions of central Arizona, including locally heavy snow over the higher terrain east of Phoenix and significant amounts of graupel and small hail over the higher desert elevations of south central Arizona. Snow levels fell to around 3500 feet early Sunday morning east of Phoenix.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A cold and slow moving Pacific low pressure system moved across Arizona during the weekend of December 3rd and 4th, and it brought locally heavy snowfall to the higher terrain of south central Arizona. The majority of heavy snow fell from the afternoon of December 3rd through the early morning hours on Sunday December 4th. According to law enforcement personnel, 3 to 4 inches of snow fell in Globe from Saturday afternoon through about 5 am Sunday morning. According to Emergency Managers, at 2 pm Saturday, approximately 2 inches of snow and ice had accumulated on power lines at the Globe Mobile Home Park. The weight of the heavy snow and ice caused power poles to fall over...and a shelter was then opened.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six to 9 inches of snow fell around central and west Flagstaff with only 3 to 5 inches in Doney Park.  Nine and a half inches of snow fell around Williams.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: About 20 inches of snow occurred at Hannagan Meadow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms developed across portions of the central Arizona deserts on the 2nd of July. The storms generated strong and damaging microburst winds in the greater Phoenix area during the afternoon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Isolated high based thunderstorms developed over the northern portions of the greater Phoenix area during the afternoon hours on the 2nd of July. The storms generated gusty damaging microburst winds which toppled 17 power poles near 32nd street and Greenway road. The wind gusts were estimated to be near 50 knots.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms developed across portions of the central Arizona deserts on the 2nd of July. The storms generated strong and damaging microburst winds in the greater Phoenix area during the afternoon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Isolated high based thunderstorms developed during the afternoon over portions of the greater Phoenix area. The storms generated strong downburst winds which tore the limbs off of trees. At 3 pm a woman was injured by a large falling tree limb at the Superstition Springs Mall in Mesa. The winds were estimated to be gusting up to 50 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms southeast of the greater Phoenix area generated strong gusty outflow winds which moved into the central Arizona deserts and created a significant dust storm during the evening hours of July 2nd. Winds gusting to 60 mph caused visibilities to drop to near zero miles in the Casa Grande area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms southeast of the greater Phoenix area produced strong outflow winds that spread to the northwest and generated a storm affecting the community of Casa Grande. A weather spotter at Val Vista and Pinal reported winds gusting to 60 mph, as well as visibility in blowing dust which was much less than one quarter of a mile.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms southeast of the greater Phoenix area generated strong gusty outflow winds which moved into the central Arizona deserts and created a significant dust storm during the evening hours of July 2nd. Winds gusting to 60 mph caused visibilities to drop to near zero miles in the Casa Grande area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High based thunderstorms to the southeast of the greater Phoenix area generated strong gusty outflow winds which moved to the northwest and generated a dense dust storm affecting the central deserts, including the community of Casa Grande. A trained weather spotter in Casa Grande indicated that dense blowing dust lowered visibility to near zero miles near the intersection of Interstate 10 and Interstate 8 during the early evening hours. Winds gusted in excess of 50 mph with this dust storm and caused significant disruption to late rush hour traffic.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Long term drought conditions continued during October with very little improvement during the month due to very little rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Following near normal Monsoon rainfall, dry and warm conditions returned to southern Arizona in October. Most areas received less than 50 percent of October's normal rainfall with a large swath receiving less than 10 percent of normal rainfall. Tucson International Airport received six hundredths of an inch for the month, while the worse drought areas of southeast Cochise County received up to fifteen hundredths of an inch. As a result, only enough precipitation fell to prevent conditions from getting much worse during the month. The lone positive was slight improvement in Greenlee County where D4 (exceptional) drought conditions dropped from 1.67 percent of the state in September to 1.24 percent in October. Local drought impacts were somewhat mitigated as the drought has persisted for several months. However, with the help of irrigation and normal Monsoon rainfall, the fall cotton crop has been reported to be fair to excellent.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong, deep, and cold low pressure center moved down the West Coast and then across Arizona.  This storm brought heavy snow to much of the area even at lower elevations. The very cold nature of this storm maximized precipitation efficiency at lower elevations while some upper elevations received less snow. This storm also brought strong winds over and northeast of the Mogollon Rim.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell across the lower elevations (below 5000 feet) of Yavapai County where heavy snow warning criteria is only 2 inches in 12 hours. Bagdad received 8 inches of snow at 3800 feet.  Paulden and 8 miles northeast of Camp Verde had 2 inches of snow. Cottonwood and Rimrock both had 3 inches of snow.  Three miles west of Dewy-Humboldt received 6.2 inches of snow. Three miles northwest of Dewy-Humboldt received 7 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High based thunderstorms over central Arizona produced strong gusty winds and dense blowing dust across central Maricopa county during the late afternoon hours on the 3rd of July. A dust storm formed and affected the area from Tonopah westward to the La Paz county line. Visibilities to near zero miles led to multiple semi truck accidents along interstate 10 west of Tonopah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High based thunderstorms across central Maricopa county generated strong gusty outflow winds during the late afternoon on July 3rd. A dust storm was generated, affecting the area from Tohopah westward to the La Paz county line. A woman living in Surprise reported 2 semi-truck accidents that occurred on Interstate 10 near Salome Road at approximately 6 pm. The accidents were a result of near zero visibility due to dense blowing dust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low along the southern California coast spread moisture into south central Arizona on the 10th. This led to scattered afternoon and evening thunderstorms, with the strongest storms located to the east of Central Phoenix. The storms produced gusty damaging winds in excess of 60 mph. Large hail and dense blowing dust also occurred across the lower south central deserts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms moved across the eastern greater Phoenix area during the evening hours on September 10th, and some of them produced large hail. A National Weather Service employee in east Mesa reported hail estimated to be one inch in diameter. No damage was reported in association with the large hail.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms with damaging winds moved across the northeastern portions of the greater Phoenix area during the evening hours on September 10th. A trained weather spotter reported that numerous large tree branches were blown down near the intersection of Scottsdale road and Westland Road, a few miles south of Carefree. Winds were estimated to be in excess of 50 knots.

In addition, the winds caused shingle damage to area homes.

Six inches of snow fell 9 miles west of Ash Fort at an elevation of around 5300 feet. Twelve and a half inches of snow fell 7 miles south of Prescott. Eleven inches of snow fell in Ponderosa Park (about 5 miles south of Prescott).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe thunderstorm development had occured in the early afternoon hours of July 5th near the Tucson, AZ area and east of Tucson. As thunderstorms dissipated and began moving northwest, outflows from these storms began producing very strong downburst winds, some in excess of 70 mph. Around 6:30pm MST, the NWS Phoenix office began receiving calls of dust moving into the Casa Grande, AZ area, about 50 miles southeast of downtown Phoenix. Upon examining radar data, it is estimated the dust storm reached a maximum height of 5000 feet to 6000 feet, the aerial coverage was close to 100 miles and the distance traveled was around 150 miles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms developed to the southeast of the greater Phoenix area during the afternoon hours on July 5. As the storms dissipated and moved towards the northwest, rain-cooled outflows picked up copious amounts of dust and dirt and generated a massive haboob which raced northwestward and across the greater Phoenix area during the evening hours. The haboob was considered to be of historic proportions; it measured at least 100 miles in length, was over one mile high and moved across the deserts at more than 50 mph. Peak wind speeds within the haboob reached 70 mph or more. Numerous trained weather spotters from across the greater Phoenix area reported visibilities down to near zero miles, in many cases visibilities were less than 50 yards. During the storm, the Buckeye fire department responded to a weather related multi-vehicle accident at Interstate 10 and Watson road in Buckeye. Thirteen people suffered minor injuries and were treated at the scene. Due to the massive amount of topsoil that was stirred up by the haboob, area Physicians and medical personnel warned that there could be a sharp rise in new Valley Fever cases among those exposed to the dust. Valley Fever is a fungal infection caused by inhalation of spores that reside in the stirred up topsoil.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated strong thunderstorms developed over the greater Phoenix area and produced damaging downburst winds during the evening hours on July 3rd. The strong winds damaged a number of trees in the community of Mesa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms developed over the eastern portions of the greater Phoenix area during the evening hours on July 3rd. The storms produced damaging downburst winds estimated to be at least 50 knots. A trained weather spotter reported numerous large tree branches were downed near Val Vista Drive and University Drive in Mesa.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large dust devil near Globe collapsed an above ground pool during the late morning hours on July 6th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An unusually strong dust devil developed west of the Globe city limits during the late morning hours of July 6th. The dust devil formed in the Central Heights area, a subdivision between Globe and Miami. According to an article in the Copper Country News, the dust devil collapsed an above ground pool. The water from the pool then brought down a concrete and rock retaining wall. The wall collapsed on top of a car, crushing and severely damaging the car.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms southeast of the greater Phoenix area generated microburst winds that moved to the northwest and into the southeast portions of the Phoenix metropolitan area. An amateur radio operator reported a peak wind gust measured at 59 mph. The strong winds generated dense blowing dust with near zero visibility, but no damage was reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms developed across northwest Pinal county during the evening hours on September 10th. Gusty outflow winds from the thunderstorms generated a dust storm over portions of the county, including the corridor from Casa Grande through Maricopa. Several trained weather spotters reported visibility at or below one quarter mile in blowing dust. At 709 pm, a spotter in Casa Grande reported visibility less than 1/8 of a mile in a dust storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold Pacific storm brought heavy snow to the higher elevations of the Mojave Desert and southern Great Basin. Locally heavy rain also occurred in the valleys.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Twelve inches of snow fell at Hualapai Mountain Lodge (4680'). Power lines and small trees were knocked down by the weight of the snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong and energetic Pacific storm system swept across Arizona with heavy rain and mountain snow. The snow level started off around 7000 feet with heavy wet snow with heavy rain below this elevation.  The snow level then dropped to around 4000 feet. The highest elevations reported over three feet of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nine inches of snow fell 14 miles northeast of Payson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong and energetic Pacific storm system swept across Arizona with heavy rain and mountain snow. The snow level started off around 7000 feet with heavy wet snow with heavy rain below this elevation.  The snow level then dropped to around 4000 feet. The highest elevations reported over three feet of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Almost nine (8.8) inches of snow fell at the Grand Canyon South Rim.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong high pressure in the upper atmosphere led to excessive heat across the southwest and south central Arizona deserts on the weekend of the 4th of July. High temperatures on Saturday, July 2nd ranged from 115 to nearly 120 degrees over the hottest lower desert, setting records in some locations such as Phoenix Sky Harbor airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong high pressure developed in the upper atmosphere over southern Arizona during the weekend of the 4th of July, which led to excessive heat over the lower deserts. High temperatures on Saturday July 2nd ranged from 115 to nearly 120 degrees over the hottest locations, setting records in the process. Phoenix Sky Harbor reached to 118 degrees, which was a new record for the date. Overnight low temperatures Sunday morning remained very high, from the mid 80s to low 90s. The low at Phoenix was a sweltering 91 degrees. An increase in clouds and humidity helped cause high temperatures to fall off Sunday however most locales still exceeded 110 degrees. An excessive heat warning was in effect for the greater Phoenix area for the duration of the weekend.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed over the south central deserts, and to the southeast of the greater Phoenix area, during the late afternoon hours on July 4th. The storms produced strong gusty outflow winds which moved to the northwest and overspread the deserts of northwestern Pinal county. The winds generated dense blowing dust, and dust storm conditions which affected communities such as Casa Grande and Arizona City.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms formed over the south central deserts, to the southeast of the greater Phoenix area, during the late afternoon hours on July 4th. Strong gusty outflow winds in excess of 40 mph produced dense blowing dust over portions of northwest Pinal county, and prompted the issuance of a dust storm warning. At 640 pm a trained weather spotter in Arizona City reported dense dust with visibility less than one quarter mile. A few spotters in the Casa Grande area reported a dirt storm with visibilities down to 200 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms developed over southeast Arizona during the afternoon hours on July 18 and they moved northwestward into the central deserts. Strong gusty winds from the thunderstorms produced a series of dust storms which affected the greater Phoenix metropolitan area as well as the communities of Coolidge and Casa Grande. Weather spotters reported visibilities down to near zero miles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms developed over southeast Arizona during  the afternoon hours on the 18th, and gusty outflow winds from the storms produced a series of dust storms which affected much of western Pinal county. Trained weather spotters in the Coolidge, Casa Grande and Stanfield areas reported visibilities lowered down to near 100 feet in dense blowing dust.  In addition, the dense blowing dust led to a 7 vehicle accident on Interstate 8 near mile marker 172. The accident occurred near 440 pm, and only minor injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike from a nearby thunderstorm caused a man to fall from a ladder in east Mesa during the afternoon on July 30th. The man was not directly struck by the lightning bolt, and suffered minor injuries in the fall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 39 year old east Mesa man was knocked off of a ladder after a lightning bolt struck nearby. The lightning bolt did not strike the man directly. The impact was near the intersection of Warner and Meridian roads. The man was awake and responding to crews arriving from the Mesa fire department and he was taken to a local hospital as a precaution.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms developed over southeast Arizona during the afternoon hours on July 18 and they moved northwestward into the central deserts. Strong gusty winds from the thunderstorms produced a series of dust storms which affected the greater Phoenix metropolitan area as well as the communities of Coolidge and Casa Grande. Weather spotters reported visibilities down to near zero miles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms developed across southeast Arizona during the afternoon hours on July 18. The storms moved into the central deserts during the early evening and generated dense blowing dust which significantly disrupted the evening rush hour over the metropolitan area. A number of trained weather spotters reported visibilities ranging from one quarter mile to near zero miles in blowing dust. A spotter at McQeen and Riggs road in Gilbert reported visibility down to  20 yards, and a spotter at 48th Street and Warner road in Ahwatukee estimated visibility at 200 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching Pacific low pressure system and associated cold front led to strong and gusty pre-frontal winds over the central deserts during the afternoon hours on October 5th. A weather station at Luke Air Force Base reported a wind gust to 61 knots; the wind gust was not associated with thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong gusty winds developed across the central deserts in advance of an approaching Pacific low pressure system during the afternoon hours on October 5th. Light rain and rain showers were present in the Phoenix area, and at 3 pm a peak wind gust of 61 knots was recorded at the Luke Air Force Base weather station. The gust was not associated with a thunderstorm, and the observation reported the presence of a squall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the early morning hours on July 25th, a lightning strike in central Phoenix caused a large branch to separate from a tree and crash down upon a carport, severely damaging the carport. No injuries were reported and there was no apparent damage to the car beneath the carport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Early morning thunderstorms produced a damaging lightning strike over Central Phoenix on July 25th. At approximately 330 am, the lightning strike caused a large branch to fall from a tree, severely damaging the carport directly underneath. No damage was reported to the 2 cars within the carport. The damage was reported by a trained NWS weather spotter located at 48th street and Mcdowell road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms developed over the central Arizona deserts during the late afternoon and evening hours on July 31st. Initially the storms produced a dust storm that moved from northwest Pinal county into the Phoenix metropolitan area. The dust storm was followed by additional thunderstorms that brought damaging microburst winds and heavy rains to the central deserts including the greater Phoenix area. Damaging microburst winds also affected the area around Casa Grande.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As reported by officers from the Casa Grande Police Department, thunderstorm microburst winds estimated to be near 80 knots severely damaged two mobile homes approximately 8 miles west of Casa Grande. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms developed over the central Arizona deserts during the late afternoon and evening hours on July 31st. Initially the storms produced a dust storm that moved from northwest Pinal county into the Phoenix metropolitan area. The dust storm was followed by additional thunderstorms that brought damaging microburst winds and heavy rains to the central deserts including the greater Phoenix area. Damaging microburst winds also affected the area around Casa Grande.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms developed in the Casa Grande area during the late afternoon hours on July 31st. A trained weather spotter 2 miles east of Casa Grande reported an estimated wind gust to 65 mph. The strong microburst winds damaged the roof on a neighboring home.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions across southern Arizona eased somewhat toward the end of December.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drastic improvements were made to drought conditions during the first half of December as widespread beneficial rain fell across the entire region. The early December rainfall coupled with above normal rainfall in November was sufficient to remove D3 (extreme) and D4 (exceptional) drought conditions from all of southern Arizona by December 20th for the first time since March.  While rainfall will be beneficial to winter annuals and crops, little improvement was seen in streams, ponds, and stock tanks. The last half of the month ended with no additional rainfall, so the concern for a dry winter remains.

A trained weather spotter reported that the strong winds blew down power lines over several houses near the Maricopa Train Station at the intersection of Taft and McDavid roads. No injuries were reported in association with the downed power lines.

While rainfall will be beneficial to winter annuals and crops, little improvement was seen in streams, ponds, and stock tanks. The last half of the month ended with no additional rainfall, so the concern for a dry winter remains.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms developed over the central Arizona deserts during the late afternoon and evening hours on July 31st. Initially the storms produced a dust storm that moved from northwest Pinal county into the Phoenix metropolitan area. The dust storm was followed by additional thunderstorms that brought damaging microburst winds and heavy rains to the central deserts including the greater Phoenix area. Damaging microburst winds also affected the area around Casa Grande.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained weather spotter in the Gilbert area reported that a large tree was downed near Higley and Baseline roads. The tree was downed by strong thunderstorm winds estimated to be around 50 knots. A former National Weather Service employee sent in a photo of another large tree that was downed at the intersection of Hunt and Gilbert roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms developed over the central Arizona deserts during the late afternoon and evening hours on July 31st. Initially the storms produced a dust storm that moved from northwest Pinal county into the Phoenix metropolitan area. The dust storm was followed by additional thunderstorms that brought damaging microburst winds and heavy rains to the central deserts including the greater Phoenix area. Damaging microburst winds also affected the area around Casa Grande.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms over central Phoenix produced damaging microburst wind gusts estimated by weather service radar to be approximately 60 mph.  As reported by several local media sources, the winds downed several power poles and damaged residential structures in the vicinity of 27th Avenue and Van Buren. The damage included backyard sheds as well as roof shingles. Damage occurred near 930 pm on the evening of July 31st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms developed over the central Arizona deserts during the late afternoon and evening hours on July 31st. Initially the storms produced a dust storm that moved from northwest Pinal county into the Phoenix metropolitan area. The dust storm was followed by additional thunderstorms that brought damaging microburst winds and heavy rains to the central deserts including the greater Phoenix area. Damaging microburst winds also affected the area around Casa Grande.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced strong damaging microburst winds over portions of Gilbert during the early evening hours on July 31st. A former National Weather Service employee reported that extensive damage was done to the Solera subdivision. Microburst wind gusts estimated to be in excess of 60 knots, damaged at least 110 trees, with 67 of the trees blown down. Additional damage was done to various structures in the community, including a block wall that was blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms developed over southwest Arizona, including the Yuma area, during the afternoon hours on September 5th. Gusty outflow winds in excess of 50 mph produced a dust storm in Yuma, and also caused damage to property. A few of the storms also produced locally heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with strong damaging winds moved through the Yuma area during the afternoon hours on September 5th.  A trained weather spotter located 5 miles north of the Fortuna Foothills reported that a box truck was blown over on Highway 95 just south of the Gila river. The wind gusts were estimated to be in excess of 50 knots. In addition, the strong microburst winds leveled an outbuilding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms developed over the central Arizona deserts during the late afternoon and evening hours on July 31st. Initially the storms produced a dust storm that moved from northwest Pinal county into the Phoenix metropolitan area. The dust storm was followed by additional thunderstorms that brought damaging microburst winds and heavy rains to the central deserts including the greater Phoenix area. Damaging microburst winds also affected the area around Casa Grande.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms produced locally heavy rain across the greater Phoenix metropolitan area during the evening hours on July 31st. Rainfall rates in excess of one inch per hour led to street flooding in a number of areas, such as near I-17 and Thunderbird roads in central Phoenix, and near Queen Creek and Ellesworth roads in Queen Creek. Reports of heavy rain and street flooding were received from several trained spotters, and they led to the issuance of an urban and small stream flood advisory for the greater Phoenix area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms developed over southeast Arizona during the afternoon hours on July 18 and they moved northwestward into the central deserts. Strong gusty winds from the thunderstorms produced a series of dust storms which affected the greater Phoenix metropolitan area as well as the communities of Coolidge and Casa Grande. Weather spotters reported visibilities down to near zero miles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with damaging microburst winds moved through the Sun City area during the early evening hours on July 18th. The strong winds damaged a number of trees at several area golf courses. According to Brian Duthu, Director of golf, five trees, including palms and pines, were lost at Sun City North course. A peak wind gust of 56 mph was measured at the North course. Four trees were lost at the Lakes West golf course. In addition to the wind, locally heavy rain occurred with the storms. 1.65 inches of rain was recorded at the North course, however no significant flooding was reported with the heavy rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms developed over southwest Arizona, including the Yuma area, during the afternoon hours on September 5th. Gusty outflow winds in excess of 50 mph produced a dust storm in Yuma, and also caused damage to property. A few of the storms also produced locally heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with strong gusty winds moved through the Yuma area during the afternoon hours on September 5th, and the strong winds generated significant blowing sand and dust. Local law enforcement personnel relayed widespread reports of visibilities below one quarter of a mile due to dense blowing dust, city-wide. A dust storm warning was issued for the Yuma area from 4 pm until 6 pm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms developed over southwest Arizona, including the Yuma area, during the afternoon hours on September 5th. Gusty outflow winds in excess of 50 mph produced a dust storm in Yuma, and also caused damage to property. A few of the storms also produced locally heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with locally heavy rainfall moved slowly across the Yuma area during the afternoon hours on September 5th. The heavy rain prompted the issuance of a small stream flood advisory for portions of west central Yuma county including the city of Fortuna Foothills. A trained weather spotter in the Yuma foothills reported 4.3 inches of rainfall, however no serious flooding was reported in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Favorable positioning of upper level high pressure brought southeast flow and a corresponding increase in moisture which resulted in afternoon and evening thunderstorms across southern Arizona. Some of the storms produced strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed a tree and ripped off a porch roof near Houghton Road and Ridgeside Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Favorable positioning of upper level high pressure brought southeast flow and a corresponding increase in moisture which resulted in afternoon and evening thunderstorms across southern Arizona. Some of the storms produced strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After strong winds peeled the roof off a volunteer organization, heavy rain caused ceiling tiles to collapse in the computer lab and over an office. Several computer units sustained water damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A favorable position of the Monsoon high pressure system and increasing moisture resulted in a second consecutive day of strong to severe thunderstorms across much of southern Arizona.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread rainfall amounts across the Tucson Metro area ranged from one half to three quarters of an inch with some spots receiving up to two inches. Several washes were running with some spilling over into local streets. This resulted in several streets being closed. There were also a stalled car on East Irvington at the Rodeo Wash near the Tucson International Airport. The driver was able to safely seek higher ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Monument Fire in the Huachuca Mountains continued to burn through July 7, charrings 30,526 acres.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Monument Fire was contained on July 7, after charring 30,526 acres (nearly 47 square miles). The fire destroyed 62 homes, four businesses, 18 outbuildings, and damaged seven homes. Nearly 10,000 people were evacuated from areas south of Sierra Vista at the peak of the fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong high pressure and dry conditions allowed daytime temperatures in Tucson to reach above 110 degrees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Well above normal temperatures were recorded in the Tucson area during the afternoon of July 2 with a high temperature reading of 111 degrees. This tied the record high for the day. As a result, a teenage boy was treated for dehydration while hiking during the late morning. Other locations across the western deserts of southern Arizona also reached heat advisory thresholds with daytime high temperatures up to 112 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from thunderstorms produced flash flooding and debris flows across the northern portion of the Wallow Fire area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Arizona Department of Transportation reported flooding on Highway 180 from mile post 419 to 425.  A motorcycle was swept away.  The driver was able to escape. Water was flowing over subdivision streets on the north side of Alpine.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Favorable positioning of upper level high pressure brought southeast flow and a corresponding increase in moisture which resulted in afternoon and evening thunderstorms across southern Arizona. Some of the storms produced strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong fast moving storm caused widespread damage across the Tucson area. Power lines and a large tree were downed near Oracle Road and Hardy Road. More power lines were downed near Park Avenue and Broadway Boulevard in midtown Tucson. Several additional trees were downed including two large trees in Vail, a 60-foot tree near Tanque Verde and Wrightstown Road, and a 100-foot tall tree onto a vehicle in northern Tucson. Two additional trees were also downed on vehicles. One fell on a car in the Fairview Manor near Miracle Mile and Fairview and another tree fell on a car near Broadway and Craycroft. The same tree also brought a light pole down on a truck. Strong winds also ripped a roof off a mobile home in southwest Tucson and destroyed several outbuildings throughout the city. In addition, winds peeled the roof off the Literacy of Life office in Mid-town. Over 20,000 Tucson Electric Power customers were without power after the storms passed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A favorable position of the Monsoon high pressure system and increasing moisture resulted in a second consecutive day of strong to severe thunderstorms across much of southern Arizona.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds resulted in significant damage around southern portions of the Tucson Metro area. Davis-Monthan Air Force Base recorded a wind gust of 67 knots (77mph) while the Tucson International Airport recorded a gust of 61 knots (70 mph). The most significant damage was sustained by businesses in South Tucson. Near Ajo Way and County Club, two industrial buildings sustained partial roof collapses with one business losing the entire roof. A Safeway Grocery Store in the Rita Ranch Village suffered a partial roof collapse. Several power poles and power lines were also downed with several blown transformers. Around 27,000 residents were without power at the peak of the storm. Several trees were downed, with trees falling on homes, cars, and into traffic creating road closures. The most significant tree damage was at the McGuire Hills Apartment Complex. Several trees were downed blocking access to many of the apartments.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A favorable position of the Monsoon high pressure system and increasing moisture resulted in a second consecutive day of strong to severe thunderstorms across much of southern Arizona.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong line of thunderstorms continued to track to the northwest across the Tucson area resulting in more significant wind damage. Several trees were downed along a line from River Road and Interstate 10 to the Gladden Farms neighborhood. In Gladden Farms, aside from trees downed, a twenty foot section of a brick wall was blown down. Additional damage was observed in Oro Valley with several trees down and outbuildings blown apart.

CZ_NAME: WHITE MOUNTAINS OF GRAHAM AND GREENLEE COUNTIES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The largest fire in Arizona history continued to burn out of control into the beginning of July. The fire was officially contained at 1800 MST on July 8. A total of 538,049 acres, including 15,407 acres in New Mexico, was burned.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Wallow Fire saw very little spread during the beginning of July thanks to very beneficial moisture. The fire was officially contained on July 8, 2011 at 1800 MST. A total of  538,049 acres were charred with around 100,000 acres charred in northern Greenlee County. Most of the structure damage and injuries were sustained north of Tucson's warning area, however, a few outbuildings were destroyed in Greenlee County. Crews stayed on scene throughout the much to monitor hot spots and work to mitigate the flash flood concerns due to the burn scar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level inverted trough along with moist southerly flow resulted in afternoon showers and thunderstorms across southeast Arizona. Some of the thunderstorms produced very heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very heavy rainfall (1.5 to 3 inches) occurred over the Monument Fire Burn area in the Huachuca Mountains resulting in significant flash flooding and damage to areas in Ash, Miller and Carr Canyons, as well as along Highway 92. Heavy rain fell only over the Ash, Miller, and Carr Canyons, but due to the burn scars, water quickly flowed down the mountain to areas that received little or no rain. Boulders, trees, mud, and propane tanks were carried by the flood waters. At least a dozen homes were damaged with most having significant structural damage. A propane tank crashed through one home's wall and into the living room leaking liquid propane. Other homes had all their lawn furniture, fountains, and outbuildings washed away. The most significant damage was sustained by Beatty's Guest Ranch in Carr Canyon. The pond was filled with over 6 feet of mud, the camp restrooms were washed down the mountain, and several units were filled with mud. One of the camp areas has also been reported as being a new channel of Miller Creek. Rail Oaks Ranch had many of the same issues as Beatty's Ranch. Further down the mountains, water and mud roared over Highway 92 and into Nicksville where several homes were damaged and filled with mud. Due to impassable roads, at least 100 people became only accessible via helicopter for nearly 3 hours. Several additional communities were evacuated. Tombstone also lost 80% of its water supply due to pipes being damaged by erosion from the rapid runoff.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture and an upper level disturbance resulted in thunderstorms that produced very heavy rainfall and strong winds in excess of 60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain occurred over the Horseshoe 2 burn scar area of the Chiricahua Mountains. The Horseshoe 2 BAER team reported three washouts on Pinery Road in the northern portions of the Chiricahua Mountains.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tropical depression located over Baja California helped bring in additional moisture to southern Arizona. An upper level disturbance triggered thunderstorms,some of which produced locally heavy rainfall and wind gusts in excess of 60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall occurred over eastern Tucson with rainfall amounts in excess of one inch in less than one hour. A swift water rescue was performed near Tanque Verde Road and Houghton Road. A motorist drove into four feet of water before the vehicle stalled, turned 180 degrees, then floated into a group of trees. Firefighters were able to pull him out safely.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tropical depression located over Baja California helped bring in additional moisture to southern Arizona. An upper level disturbance triggered thunderstorms,some of which produced locally heavy rainfall and wind gusts in excess of 60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew off the roof of an industrial building in Sierra Vista. Part of the roof knocked over a brick wall. Part of one wall of the building also collapsed. Several trees and branches were also downed in the same area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mesoscale convective vortex left over from an overnight mesoscale convective system over Sonora, Mexico helped initiate thunderstorms across southern Arizona. Some of these storms were able to produce winds in excess of 60 miles per hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds flipped over a trailer home and downed several trees west of Santa Rosa.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A favorable position of the Monsoon high pressure system and increasing moisture resulted in a second consecutive day of strong to severe thunderstorms across much of southern Arizona.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter in Saddlebrooke reported significant thunderstorm wind damage. Several large, established trees were downed causing minor roof damage, and brick walls blown down. In Avra Valley, mesquite trees were snapped at the base while large limbs were broke off others.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure centered in northern New Mexico allowed for favorable monsoon flow and deep moisture across southern Arizona, which resulted in severe afternoon and evening thunderstorms and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed eight power poles along Oracle Road near Magee Road,  resulting in the closure of some lanes of Oracle Road. Significant tree damage was along Campbell Avenue between Glenn Street and Grant Road. A tree was also downed on a vehicle in a business parking lot near Ina Road and La Cholla Boulevard. A trained spotter measured a wind gust of 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture and an upper level disturbance resulted in thunderstorms that produced very heavy rainfall and strong winds in excess of 60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain fell over portions of the Wallow Fire burned White Mountains resulting in flash flooding near Hannagan Meadow. A mud slide from rapid runoff forced the closure of Highway 191 from mile marker 232 to 239.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture and high pressure centered over northern New Mexico resulted in prime conditions for afternoon and evening severe thunderstorms across southeast Arizona. Thunderstorms produced large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Willcox Police reported several minutes of one inch diameter hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Favorable southeast flow and abundant moisture resulted in severe thunderstorms producing winds in excess of 60 miles per hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Bisbee-Douglas International Airport ASOS recorded a 53 knot (61 mph) wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure centered in northern New Mexico allowed for favorable monsoon flow and deep moisture across southern Arizona, which resulted in severe afternoon and evening thunderstorms and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread heavy rain ranging from one half to one and a half inches in Oro Valley and northern Tucson resulted in flash flooding. A woman had to be rescued from a wash as she was swept away downstream. She was taken to a local hospital and treated for hypothermia. Several area roads were closed including a large section of Magee Road between La Cholla Road and Oracle Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure centered in northern New Mexico allowed for favorable monsoon flow and deep moisture across southern Arizona, which resulted in severe afternoon and evening thunderstorms and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter reported several one inch diameter hail stones.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure centered in northern New Mexico allowed for favorable monsoon flow and deep moisture across southern Arizona, which resulted in severe afternoon and evening thunderstorms and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported hail as large as quarters covering the ground at La Canada Drive and Orange Grove Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure centered in northern New Mexico allowed for favorable monsoon flow and deep moisture across southern Arizona, which resulted in severe afternoon and evening thunderstorms and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Mesonet site recorded a wind gust of 51 knots (59 mph) at Tangerine Road and Thornydale Road. Several large trees were also downed in the Eagle Crest neighborhood in Saddlebrooke.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure centered in northern New Mexico allowed for favorable monsoon flow and deep moisture across southern Arizona, which resulted in severe afternoon and evening thunderstorms and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported several palo verde trees downed in Sahuarita.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An increase in low level moisture from the Gulf of California combined with an unstable air mass to trigger thunderstorms that produced damaging winds and locally heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds flipped over a truck over near Interstate 19.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An increase in low level moisture from the Gulf of California combined with an unstable air mass to trigger thunderstorms that produced damaging winds and locally heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall fell across South Tucson with rainfall amounts ranging from one and a half inches to two inches. Significant street and neighborhood flooding occurred along Old Vail Connection from Wilmot Road to Interstate 19.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Well above normal precipitable water values and an upper level disturbance triggered thunderstorms that produced very heavy rain and subsequent flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains produced rainfall amounts greater than one inch in mid town Tucson. This resulted in several intersections being closed along First Street between Speedway Blvd. and Grant Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture and high pressure centered over northern New Mexico resulted in prime conditions for afternoon and evening severe thunderstorms across southeast Arizona. Thunderstorms produced large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported an estimated wind gust of 52 knots (60 mph) in northern Sahuarita. Several large trees and a few power poles were downed. The most significant damage was sustained on Pima Mine Road near Interstate 19 where a pole fell onto a moving vehicle. The driver sustained minor injuries and was held overnight for observation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Well above normal precipitable water values and an upper level disturbance triggered thunderstorms that produced very heavy rain and subsequent flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced an inch of rain in less than 30 minutes over the Horseshoe 2 burn area of the Chiricahua Mountains. This caused a flash flood and significant debris flow near Portal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Well above normal precipitable water remained over the region, which resulted in thunderstorms that developed to produce heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between one and three inches of heavy rain occurred over the Horseshoe 2 burn scar in the eastern Chiricahua Mountains. The result was flash flooding and a debris flow across Cave Creek Road and 42A Forest Road. The Research Center also noted the normally dry creek near their building was roaring with large amounts of debris in the water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure located over northern New Mexico, deep moisture, and unstable conditions resulted in afternoon and evening thunderstorms, some of which produced damaging winds and heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm microburst winds downed 20 power poles in Sahuarita along La Canada Road. Several additional trees and branches were also downed.

CZ_NAME: UPPER SANTA CRUZ RIVER AND ALTAR VALLEYS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Long term drought conditions continued in July with some improvement due to beneficial monsoon rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: July brought much needed monsoon moisture to southern Arizona, but rainfall amounts were not sufficient to drastically reduce the severity of the drought conditions. Drought conditions were unchanged to start the month with some reduction of D3 and D4 conditions by the end of the month. The Tucson area was downgraded from D3 to D2 conditions as well as portions of western Pima County, southeast Pinal County, and northwest Graham County. Extreme southwest Cochise County was downgraded from D4 conditions to D3 conditions. Most areas ended the month with below normal rainfall, with only portions of western Cochise County and northern Greenlee County experiencing above normal rainfall. Above normal rainfall over the Monument Fire area resulted in significant flash flooding and additional damage to the area. According to state officials, range conditions continued to be extremely poor with dry stock tanks. Water was being hauled to fill stock ponds and tanks, and feed had to be brought in to supplement poor forage conditions. Wildlife was also being impacted due to lack of water and available forage. The Arizona Drought Watch reported severe impacts in the Upper Gila River Valley in southern Greenlee County. Unusually low water levels were observed in reservoirs, lakes, and ponds. Poor water quality due to low levels and low flows were also reported. Cattle were being supplemented, but herds were still losing fat levels.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper level disturbance rotating northward out of Mexico along with abundant moisture set the stage for afternoon thunderstorms that produced winds in excess of 60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A microburst caused localized damage near 22nd and Wilmot in Tucson. A roof was peeled off a house, with part of it landing 22nd Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper level disturbance rotating northward out of Mexico along with abundant moisture set the stage for afternoon thunderstorms that produced winds in excess of 60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A microburst over downtown Willcox caused damage along Haskell Street. A tin roof was blown off one building while part of a roof was peeled off another. Several trees were downed including a large tree in Railroad Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance and adequate moisture allowed for afternoon and evening showers and thunderstorms, some of which produced winds in excess of 60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew through northwest portions of the Tucson area uprooting several trees from Ina Road and Interstate 10 to the Continental Ranch subdivision at Cortaro Road and Silverbell Road. Tree limbs were downed onto Interstate 10 creating traffic delays. Several trees were also downed in Arizona Pavilion Shopping Center. One tree in the Continental Ranch neighborhood knocked a brick wall down. Also, a trampoline was blown into a house causing minor damage to the home.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture and an upper level disturbance resulted in thunderstorms that produced very heavy rainfall and strong winds in excess of 60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed several power poles and lines in Douglas. A trained spotter measured a wind gust of 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms developed across portions of south central Arizona, including the greater Phoenix area, during the late afternoon and evening hours on August 18th. The storms produced strong gusty outflow winds which caused a dust storm over the lower deserts, including portions of the greater Phoenix area and western Pinal counties. Damaging microburst winds and locally heavy rain accompanied the strong storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms moved across the greater Phoenix metropolitan area during the early evening hours of August 18th. According to local broadcast media sources, a wind gust measured at 62 mph occurred at Zuni Hills elementary school, located about 3 miles northwest of Sun City. No damage was reported at the school.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Increasing moisture interacted with an upper level shortwave trough to produce afternoon showers and thunderstorms. Some of the storms produced heavy rainfall and strong winds.
Increasing moisture interacted with an upper level shortwave trough to produce afternoon showers and thunderstorms. Some of the storms produced heavy rainfall and strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall resulted in two, single car accidents on Interstate 10 between Ina Road and Orange Grove Road due to hydroplaning. One person was taken to the hospital with minor injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure located over southern California interacted with increasing moisture across southern Arizona to produce strong to severe afternoon and evening thunderstorms. Some of the storms produced heavy rainfall, strong winds, and large hail.

Low pressure located over southern California interacted with increasing moisture across southern Arizona to produce strong to severe afternoon and evening thunderstorms. Some of the storms produced heavy rainfall, strong winds, and large hail.
