A strong cold front brought high northerly winds to much of the Mojave Desert and southern Great Basin. Damage occurred in many areas, and there was also isolated heavy snow in the high mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This gust occurred at the Bullhead City airport (KIFP). The high winds continued past midnight, causing damage in December.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Long term drought conditions continued during October with very little improvement during the month due to very little rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Following near normal Monsoon rainfall, dry and warm conditions returned to southern Arizona in October. Most areas received less than 50 percent of October's normal rainfall with a large swath receiving less than 10 percent of normal rainfall. Tucson International Airport received six hundredths of an inch for the month, while the worse drought areas of southeast Cochise County received up to fifteen hundredths of an inch. As a result, only enough precipitation fell to prevent conditions from getting much worse during the month. The lone positive was slight improvement in Greenlee County where D4 (exceptional) drought conditions dropped from 1.67 percent of the state in September to 1.24 percent in October. Local drought impacts were somewhat mitigated as the drought has persisted for several months. However, with the help of irrigation and normal Monsoon rainfall, the fall cotton crop has been reported to be fair to excellent.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moist southeasterly flow interacted with a weather disturbance lifting north from Sonora, Mexico.  This resulted in afternoon and evening thunderstorms, some of which produced heavy rain and strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall of 2 and 3 inches near and east of Sahuarita caused the closure of  several roads including Alvernon, Comsoft, Wilmot and Kolb. Swift water rescues from vehicles were performed near the intersections of Delgado and Davis Roads and Wilmot and Sahuarita Roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moist southeasterly flow interacted with a weather disturbance lifting north from Sonora, Mexico.  This resulted in afternoon and evening thunderstorms, some of which produced heavy rain and strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced a swath of large hail from Green Valley northeastward several miles out of town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moist southeasterly flow interacted with a weather disturbance lifting north from Sonora, Mexico.  This resulted in afternoon and evening thunderstorms, some of which produced heavy rain and strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms produced large hail in Sierra Vista and Hereford.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moist southeasterly flow interacted with a weather disturbance lifting north from Sonora, Mexico.  This resulted in afternoon and evening thunderstorms, some of which produced heavy rain and strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a home in the 36000 block of Stoney Flower causing significant damage. Twenty-five firefighters responded and were on scene for two hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front brought high northerly winds to much of the Mojave Desert and southern Great Basin. Damage occurred in many areas, and there was also isolated heavy snow in the high mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds which began before midnight on November 30th continued into December, damaging two roofs and one porch, breaking a window, blowing down power lines, and blowing out one transformer in Bullhead City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front brought high northerly winds to much of the Mojave Desert and southern Great Basin. Damage occurred in many areas, and there was also isolated heavy snow in the high mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A total of 12 inches of snow fell at Hualapai Mountain, and also at Diamond M Ranch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced heavy rain near Nutrioso over the Wallow Fire Burn Area. This caused flash flooding and debris/mud flow across Highway 180.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain and flash flooding caused mud and debris flows to cross Highway 180 at mile post 416 near Nutrioso.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system in northern Arizona interacted with abundant moisture to produce showers and thunderstorms across southern Arizona. Some of the storms produced large hail, torrential rains, and strong winds. The Tucson International Airport recorded the fifth wettest day in recorded history.

Strong low pressure tracked through the desert Southwest resulting in strong southwest winds across southern Arizona. As a result, several localized dust storms occurred.  One of these caused a fatal accident on Interstate 10.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extremely dry soils and strong southwest winds caused localized dust storms along Interstate 10 in southern Pinal County. One dust storm reduced visibility to less than one quarter of a mile causing a fatal 16-vehicle accident that closed the westbound lanes of Interstate 10 for several hours. A Texas man was killed in the accident and a total of 15 people were injured, two seriously. The dust storm prohibited air traffic, so the critically wounded were escorted by ambulance to the nearest hospital.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Increasing moisture interacted with an upper level shortwave trough to produce afternoon showers and thunderstorms. Some of the storms produced heavy rainfall and strong winds.
A thunderstorm produced heavy rain and street flooding near Prescott Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain exceeding rates of 2 inches an hour, produced heavy local street flooding 2 miles north of Prescott Valley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced heavy rain and street flooding near Prescott Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain (1.35) and hail up to 1/2 inch was reported 1 mile east of Highway 89 in Chino Valley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure center over northwestern Arizona brought abundant moisture and dynamics to central and eastern parts of northern Arizona. This lead to thunderstorms producing a tornado, large hail, heavy rain, and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized (1 inch diameter) hail covered the ground 1 mile south of Schnebly Hill Road Exit on I-17.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure center over northwestern Arizona brought abundant moisture and dynamics to central and eastern parts of northern Arizona. This lead to thunderstorms producing a tornado, large hail, heavy rain, and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An official from the Sedona Police Department reported that creeks in the Tlaquepaque area had overflowed. The flood water covered road ways and forced the closure of several roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure center over northwestern Arizona brought abundant moisture and dynamics to central and eastern parts of northern Arizona. This lead to thunderstorms producing a tornado, large hail, heavy rain, and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An official with the Lake Powell Tribal Park reported flash flooding of Rattle Snake and Owl Canyons.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moist southerly flow interacted with the mountains of southern Arizona to produce isolated thunderstorms. Some of the storms produced strong winds and heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds resulted in several downed trees along Fry Blvd. in Sierra Vista. Some of the trees were blocking the driving lanes on Fry Blvd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure center over northwestern Arizona brought abundant moisture and dynamics to central and eastern parts of northern Arizona. This lead to thunderstorms producing a tornado, large hail, heavy rain, and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law Enforcement officials reported flooding on Zalesky Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Enough left over moisture interacted with a shortwave trough to produce isolated to scattered showers and thunderstorms across southern Arizona. Some of the storms produced heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An intense thunderstorm produced torrential rainfall along Interstate 10 between mile markers 285 and 292 near the Pima County and Cochise County line. The Arizona Department of Transportation worked six accidents involving a total of nine vehicles resulting in seven non-life-threatening injuries. Arizona DOT reported that heavy rainfall, zero visibility, and hydroplaning were to blame. Interstate 10 was partially closed for about 30 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Long term drought conditions continued during September with some improvement during the beginning of the month, mostly across eastern Pima County. Dry conditions returned the later half of the month as the Monsoon began to wind down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The month of September started off with above average rainfall across eastern Pima County and well as western Cochise County. However, the rest of the area continued to receive below normal rainfall and above normal temperatures. Tucson International Airport received the fifth highest rainfall amount in recorded history on September 15 of 2.84 inches. However, locations nearby received a tenth of an inch or less. This allowed the Tucson area, and northeast Pima County to be dropped from extreme drought conditions (D3) to severe drought conditions (D2). Extreme drought condition areas were lowered by almost 4 percent by the end of the month. However, due to the lack of rainfall across eastern Cochise, Graham, and Greenlee Counties, exceptional drought conditions (D4) expanded across southern Greenlee County. Most areas received less than 50 percent of the average rainfall, while areas in eastern Pima County and western Cochise County received greater than 300 percent of normal. Drought impacts across the state actually lessened in September as dry conditions have continued for several months with little change from month to month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced one inch hail in Munds Park and a mud slide in Oak Creek Canyon.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall resulted in flooded streets and washes near Red Rock. Two elderly women attempted to drive through the flooded intersection at Park Link Dr. and Pecan Rd. when their vehicle stalled. Avra Valley Fire Dept. responded and rescued the women and a dog with water up to the windows.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moist southeasterly flow interacted with a weather disturbance lifting north from Sonora, Mexico.  This resulted in afternoon and evening thunderstorms, some of which produced heavy rain and strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed several trees on Tucson's south side. One tree fell on a truck and another onto an apartment building. The truck was totaled but the building only received minor damage. In addition, strong winds ripped the air conditioning unit and tiles off the roof of the Southgate Academy Charter School.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law Enforcement officials reported an ongoing swift water rescue on Spring Creek.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm survey revealed that significant forest damage occurred along the Humpherys Trail near the Snowbowl Ski Area around 10,000 feet elevation. Preliminary rating was EF2.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Increasing moisture interacted with an upper level shortwave trough to produce afternoon showers and thunderstorms. Some of the storms produced heavy rainfall and strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed several trees in the Catalina Foothills. A large mesquite tree blocked the road at Rudasil and Tiburon Roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Increasing moisture interacted with an upper level shortwave trough to produce afternoon showers and thunderstorms. Some of the storms produced heavy rainfall and strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds occurred on Tucson's south side for the second day in a row, downing numerous power poles, electric lines, street signs, and trees. As many as 25,000 were left without power. One power pole was downed on a trailer leading to the evacuation of the trailer park near East Drexel and South Country Club Roads. Another Tucson resident woke up to a power pole entering the dwelling. Part of a roof was also blown off a residence on Valencia Road, blowing across the road into another home damaging the roof.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall resulted in rapid runoff and flash flooding on Tucson's south side. Several streets were flooded around the Tucson International Airport and a swift water rescue was conducted at Old Nogales and Old Vail Roads. The most significant damage was sustained by the Marshall Home for Men near Interstate 10 and Interstate 19 where a small wash overflowed into the business, resulting in significant water damage to the interior. The force of the water also broke through the front door.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Increasing moisture interacted with an upper level shortwave trough to produce afternoon showers and thunderstorms. Some of the storms produced heavy rainfall and strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall with amounts between one and two inches resulted in flash flooding across a wide swath of the Tucson area. Several swift water rescues were conducted with two near the 3800 block of West Goret Road in the Tucson Mountains. Another swift water rescue was conducted in the 9400 block of Hartman Lane, where a motorcyclist had to be rescued with a rope and flotation device.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure located over southern California interacted with increasing moisture across southern Arizona to produce strong to severe afternoon and evening thunderstorms. Some of the storms produced heavy rainfall, strong winds, and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed several trees, including one with a three foot diameter trunk, near the community of Pima.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Increasing moisture interacted with an upper level shortwave trough to produce afternoon showers and thunderstorms. Some of the storms produced heavy rainfall and strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was observed near Vail. It extended about a quarter of the way from the cloud base to the ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system in northern Arizona interacted with abundant moisture to produce showers and thunderstorms across southern Arizona. Some of the storms produced large hail, torrential rains, and strong winds. The Tucson International Airport recorded the fifth wettest day in recorded history.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extremely heavy rainfall across southern Tucson resulted in significant wash and stream flooding downstream from Tucson. Overflowing washes toppled a wall in the Rancho Sahuarita subdivision in Sahuarita resulting in damage to 15 homes and a muddy mess. Water up to one foot was observed in some of the homes.  The raging Santa Cruz River also claimed the life of a homeless man that was living under the Grant Road Bridge. Rescuers spotted the man holding onto the bridge pillar, but before they were able to reach him he lost his grip and was swept down the river. His body was found the next day near the Ina Road bridge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe thunderstorm development had occured in the early afternoon hours of July 5th near the Tucson, AZ area and east of Tucson. As thunderstorms dissipated and began moving northwest, outflows from these storms began producing very strong downburst winds, some in excess of 70 mph. Around 6:30pm MST, the NWS Phoenix office began receiving calls of dust moving into the Casa Grande, AZ area, about 50 miles southeast of downtown Phoenix. Upon examining radar data, it is estimated the dust storm reached a maximum height of 5000 feet to 6000 feet, the aerial coverage was close to 100 miles and the distance traveled was around 150 miles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very strong thunderstorms developed southeast of the greater Phoenix area and north of Tucson during the late afternoon hours on July 5th. The storms produced gusty outflow winds in excess of 50 mph which moved to the northwest and across northwest Pinal county. The strong winds generated dense blowing dust which turned into a massive haboob more than one mile high. A number of weather spotters reported visibilities down to near zero miles, with winds between 35 and 55 mph. At 645 pm, a trained spotter near Arizona City reported zero visibility with winds gusting up to 55 mph. Another spotter in Johnson Ranch reported visibility down to 150 feet with winds 30 mph or greater. The massive haboob significantly disrupted traffic across northwest Pinal county during the early evening hours.

CZ_NAME: WHITE MOUNTAINS OF GRAHAM AND GREENLEE COUNTIES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The largest wildfire in Arizona state history started as an unattended campfire on May 29, 2011. Extreme drought conditions and strong winds allowed for this fire to quickly become out of control. By the time the fire was 100% contained during the beginning of July, 538,049 acres had been charred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Wallow Fire continued to burn out of control during the entire month of June. While the brunt of the fire was burning in neighboring Apache County, the fire charred nearly 100,000 acres in northern Greenlee County. The Hannagan Meadow Lodge was evacuated and closed, followed by the closure of Highway 191. Most of the outbuildings and homes damaged were in Apache County, with only a few outbuildings destroyed in northern Greenlee County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered high based thunderstorms developed across southwestern Arizona, including the Yuma area, during the evening hours on August 27th. The storms generated gusty wind in excess of 58 mph which produced some damage across portions of Yuma.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed in the Yuma area during the late evening hours on August 27th, and the storms generated strong and gusty winds. The official ASOS weather station in Yuma measured a peak wind gust of 50 knots at 1030 pm. The gusty winds also generated areas of dense blowing sand and dust; the Yuma observation reported visibility down to one quarter of a mile in blowing dust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unseasonably strong high pressure developed over the desert southwest during late August. Coupled with a rather dry air mass, this led to a protracted heat wave over the lower deserts of southwest and southcentral Arizona beginning on August 22nd and continuing through the end of the month. The high temperatures at Phoenix ranged from 113 to 117 degrees from Monday through Friday, and the high heat led to at least 2 deaths in the greater Phoenix area. Overnight minimum temperatures remained very high each night, and this led to an excessive heat warning that ran 24/7 from Monday morning through the end of August.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong high pressure and a dry airmass led to excessive heat over the lower deserts of Arizona, starting Monday August 22nd and continuing through the end of the month. The hottest days during the heat wave were the first five, Monday through Friday, when the highs at Phoenix ranged from 113 to 117 degrees. The five consecutive days at or above 113 ties for the third longest streak of days with a high of 113 or greater. The 117 degree maximum on Friday August 26 not only broke the record for the date, but was the latest 117 degree maximum ever recorded, and the highest temperature ever recorded for the month of August. The extreme head led to the deaths of two people in the Phoenix area. A 70 year old woman was found dead Wednesday morning in her Surprise home after her air conditioner failed and the temperature rose to 107 degrees. Surprise Fire Captain Renee Hambin indicated that the cause of death was obviously related to the excessive heat inside her home. Additionally, a 75 year old Glendale man was found dead in his home on Thursday August 25th. Police went to his home as part of a welfare check and found him dead with the air conditioner blowing hot air and the temperature in excess of 100 degrees. Police Sgt. Brent Coombs said that the heat was definitely a factor in the man's death.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong high pressure and very dry air mass resulted in abnormally hot daytime temperatures across all of southern Arizona.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Well above normal daytime temperatures settled over southern Arizona on June 27 and 28. On June 27th, a record 112 degrees was recorded at the Tucson ASOS. Several valley locations below four thousand feet reached heat criteria levels. Heat criteria was also reached in the higher elevations. The Chiricahua National Monument (5133 ft) recorded a high temperature of 102 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoon moisture caused scattered thunderstorms across northern Arizona caused heavy rain, flash flooding,  a rock slide, and a funnel cloud.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding was reported in the Greer area. Drainages were flowing 20 to 50 feet wide and 12 to 18 inches deep in Bulter Canyon and East Fork of the Little Colorado River. Water was flowing across the southern portion of East Fork Road. The Little Colorado River was up 18 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another major wildfire began as a human caused fire on June 12 in a remote location of the Huachuca Mountains near the International Border. The fire eventually charred 30,526 acres and was contained on July 7.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme drought conditions, coupled with adequate fuels and strong winds allowed a human caused fire to quickly spread out of control. The fire was first reported on June 12 by Border Patrol west of Hereford near the Mexico Border. Favorable weather conditions, rugged terrain, and extremely dry fuels allowed the fire to quickly spread out of control, growing 3,159 acres on the first day. By the end of the month, nearly 30,000 acres had burned. Strong southwest winds were funneled through the canyons of the Huachuca Mountains on July 14 allowing for a strong fire run into the city limits of Nicksville. Only a few homes and outbuildings were damaged or destroyed. A more significant run occurred of July 16 and 17 as strong winds pushed the fire into southern Sierra Vista resulting in several homes, outbuildings, and business either being damaged or destroyed. Several homes and outbuildings located in the Huachuca Mountains were also damaged or destroyed. A bulldozer sparked a fire on the army base of Ft Huachuca on June 18. That fire was quickly contained, but residents were evacuated. Thousands of residents were also evacuated from southern Sierra Vista during the blaze.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced flooding below the southern section of the Schultz Fire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from a thunderstorm caused flash flooding and debris flow over Paintbrush Lane below the Schutlz Fire burn area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Long term drought conditions continued during the month of June, expanding in coverage and intensity as southern Arizona received little to no rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extremely dry conditions continued in June with no rainfall to begin the month. The first Drought Monitor issued during June expanded D3 conditions to include all of Graham, Greenlee, and Cochise Counties. It also introduced the first D4 exceptional drought condition into extreme eastern Cochise County and extreme southern Greenlee County. The D4 drought areas had received no significant rainfall since October, 2010. The last measurable rainfall at Bisbee-Douglas International Airport was on February 27, 2011. The airport finally received two hundredths of an inch on June 29 bringing an end to 122 consecutive days without measurable rainfall. The Tucson Airport also went 81 days with no measurable rainfall, making it the fourth longest dry spell on record. ||The large Wallow Fire and Horseshoe 2 Fire prompted the U.S. Forest Service to close Coronado National Forest on June 10. However, a new fire, the Monument Fire, started on June 12 in the Huachuca Mountains that threatened the city of Sierra Vista. By the end of the month, these three massive fires had charred more than three-quarter of a million acres. ||The Arizona Drought Watch continued to report serious watershed conditions all across southern Arizona in June. Unusually low water levels in reservoirs, lakes, and ponds with unusually low flows in streams rivers, and springs. This resulted in unusually low forage and vegetation range productivity, along with poor forage nutrition and increased toxicity of forage. Due to drought-related wildfires, hundreds of thousands of acres of range land forest was lost. Agriculture impacts included limited cattle grazing which in turn caused a leaner herd that needed to be sent to market prematurely because of a lack of food and water sources. Tourism was also impacted from the closure of national and state parks due to wildfire concerns. Native reptile and amphibian species were also harmed as a result of dried up ponds and streams.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms developed over southwest Arizona, including the Yuma area, during the afternoon hours on September 5th. Gusty outflow winds in excess of 50 mph produced a dust storm in Yuma, and also caused damage to property. A few of the storms also produced locally heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with strong, damaging winds moved through the Yuma foothills during the afternoon hours on September 5th. At 425 pm, a trained weather spotter estimated a wind gust to be 60 mph. The spotter, located a few miles west of the Fortuna Foothills, also reported that a roof was partially blown off of a shed by the high winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Locust Fire burned 364 acres on a steep hillside hillside just west of the community of White River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Locust fire was reported at 1145 AM on the west side of the Community of White River. The fire was human caused.  The main impact of the fire was the removal of vegetation and ground cover on the steep hillside above 137 homes. This would lead to flooding issues during the 2011 rainy season and beyond.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Willow Fire was human caused and burned about 213 acres with 292 people assigned to the fire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Willow Fire was human caused on June 19th approximately 13 miles northwest of Forest Lakes, AZ.  The fire was turned back to the local district at 600 PM on June 23rd. Full containment was achieved by the end of June. Crews still patrolled the fire into mid July.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Engineer Fire was human caused on the Camp Navajo Army Depot on June 2 during Red Flag conditions. The fire grew to over 600 acres.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Engineer Fire was human caused on the Camp Navajo Army Depot base.  Containment was achieved at 601 acres on Tuesday June 7th at 600 PM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Wash fire was human caused about 8 miles NE of Heber-Overgaard on June 23rd.  It burned a total of 1,955 acres and 151 people were employed to combat the fire.  The fire transition to local unit occurred on 6/27/2011.  Full containment was late on June 29th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Wash Fire burned about 1,955 acres 	8 miles NE of Heber-Overgaard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Wallow Fire started on May 29, 2011 from an abandoned campfire in the Bear Wallow Wilderness in northern Greenlee County about 24 miles southwest of Alpine, Arizona.  The fire spread into Apache County on June first. Red Flag conditions caused fire spreads of 8 and 12 miles on different days.   This fire eventually burned 538,049 total acres in eastern Arizona and western New Mexico (15,397 acres in New Mexico).  In Apache County, 313,103 acres burned in Apache County (WFO Flagstaff's CWA). Containment (the fire did not spread any more) was achieved by the end of June and the fire was controlled (declared out) on July 13th. Over 6,000 people were employed to fight this fire at a cost of over $100,000,000!  It became the largest fire in Arizona history. In all, there were 32 residences destroyed, 5 residences damaged, 4 commercial properties destroyed, 36 outbuildings destroyed, 1 damaged outbuilding, and one vehicle destroyed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Wallow Fire entered Apache County around 200 PM on June first.  The fire burned a total of 313,103 acres in Apache County. The fire did not spread after June 30th (it was contained). The fire was declared out (controlled) on July 13th. It cost an estimated 58 million dollars to fight the fire in Apache County alone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Early morning thunderstorms moved through the Tucson area breaking an 81 day streak of no rain at the Tucson Airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Early morning thunderstorms produced brief gusty winds across South Tucson and resulted in one tree being uprooted, and another tree toppling over onto an apartment complex roof at Mission Road and Drexel Road. The Apartment complex suffered minor tile damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced heavy rain over the Schultz Burn Area which lead to flash flooding down hill.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain gages across the Schultz Fire Area received between 0.59 and 2.68 of rain. Several roads were closed due to flash flooding and debris flows in Hutchison Acres and Timberline neighborhoods downstream from the Schultz Burn Area. Eighteen to 24 inches of water was flowing across Pi��on Road and other roads in the Timberline area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms brought heavy rain to the Western Mogollon Rim and portions of northeast Arizona. Heavy rain caused flash flooding that closed at least one road near Kayenta.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain (1.50 inches) was reported in east Flagstaff.  Rain gages in the Schultz Fire burned area reported between 0.39 and 0.83 inches of rain. Most of the water flowing off the burned area remained in ditches with minor street flooding. Three streets experienced higher flows and large amounts of debris were deposited on the roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced heavy rain over the Wallow Burn Area causing flash flooding and debris/mud flows.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A business in Greer reported heavy rain (0.50) with water flowing down the street. A Mesonet station at Hay Lake recorded 0.87 inches of rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A favorable position of the Monsoon high pressure system and increasing moisture resulted in a second consecutive day of strong to severe thunderstorms across much of southern Arizona.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tohono O'odham Nation Emergency Manager reported thunderstorm winds caused structural damage to two homes in Lukeville. One home suffered minor roof damage while the other home suffered a partial roof collapse. Another home was damaged in the Gu Vo District of the Tohono O'odham. The home had damage to the roof and walls.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Long term drought conditions continued in August although minor improvement due to beneficial monsoon rain, occurred across parts of Cochise, Graham and Greenlee Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After beneficial rainfall in July and early August, the entire month of August ended with below normal rainfall except across parts of western Cochise County. The result was that D3 extreme drought conditions retreated eastward, completely exiting Santa Cruz County, by the middle of the month, but expanded even farther westward across Pima and Pinal Counties by the end of August. D4 exceptional drought conditions did show an overall decrease in state coverage from 4.83% to 1.35%, with only extreme eastern Cochise County, southern Greenlee County, and extreme southeast Graham County remaining in exceptional drought conditions in southeast Arizona. The most impacted watersheds during August were the San Simon watershed (2nd percentile for rainfall) and the Santa Cruz watershed (2nd percentile for rainfall), which received very little rainfall. July's rainfall brought an end to the fire season so many recreational areas were reopened in August. The state and federal forest services conducted large scale seeding operations across the burn scar areas attempting to mitigate the flash flooding concerns. Very few ranchers were still holding onto cattle, so agriculture impacts were somewhat lessened during the month. Cotton and vegetable crops were reported as being fair to excellent as irrigation was being maximized.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms brought heavy rain to the Western Mogollon Rim and portions of northeast Arizona. Heavy rain caused flash flooding that closed at least one road near Kayenta.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: US Highway 160 near Kayenta was close for about an hour due to heavy rain and flash flooding at low water crossings near Kayenta.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An increase in low level moisture from the Gulf of California combined with an unstable air mass to trigger thunderstorms that produced damaging winds and locally heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tohono O'odham Nation Emergency Managers reported damaging winds in the Vaya Chin and Hickiwan villages. Several trailers and a home was damaged. One trailer received major structural damage and another trailer was completely destroyed. The homeowners were not home at the time. A vacant trailer was also destroyed. In addition, a traditional style home received significant roof damage and a HUD (Housing and Urban Development) home also received roof damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistent upper level Pacific low pressure system located along the southern California coast led to scattered thunderstorms over southwest Arizona during the afternoon and evening hours on September 12th. Some of the storms produced strong and gusty winds which produced a dust storm in the Yuma area. Large hail was also generated by the stronger storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced large hail in the Yuma area during the afternoon hours on September 12th. A trained weather spotter reported hail as big as three quarters of an inch in diameter at the intersection of Avenue 6E and 32nd Street, near Araby. No damage was reported with the large hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistent upper level Pacific low pressure system located along the southern California coast led to scattered thunderstorms over southwest Arizona during the afternoon and evening hours on September 12th. Some of the storms produced strong and gusty winds which produced a dust storm in the Yuma area. Large hail was also generated by the stronger storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms to the southeast of Yuma generated strong and gusty outflow winds which moved into the Yuma area and generated a significant dust storm. Several trained weather spotters reported visibility at or below one quarter of a mile in blowing dust and sand. At about 330 pm, a weather service employee 8 miles southeast of Somerton reported visibility down to 1/8 of a mile in dense blowing dust and sand. The dust storm was a significant hazard to motorists in the Yuma area during the afternoon rush hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm dropped heavy rain over the area that was burned by the Schultz Fire.  Heavy runoff produced flash flooding below the burned area in Hutchinson Acres and along Campbell Avenue.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter in Hutchinson Acres reported moderate flooding of roads and washes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level Pacific low located along the southern California coast spread moisture into south central Arizona on September 11th. The moisture, combined with an unstable airmass, allowed scattered strong thunderstorms to develop during the afternoon and evening hours. Damaging winds in excess of 50 mph, dense blowing dust and large hail were all reported with these thunderstorms. A dust storm was generated across portions of the greater Phoenix metropolitan area during the early evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with damaging microburst winds moved through the town of Maricopa during the late afternoon hours on September 11th. A trained weather spotter reported that strong thunderstorm winds, estimated to be in excess of 40 knots, blew down a segment of a brick wall. Additionally, it was reported in a local newspaper that approximately 80 trees were blown down at the El Rancho El Dorado subdivision and the Duke golf course in Maricopa. The trees varied in size with the largest being about 10 inches in diameter. Peak winds that downed the trees may have exceeded 50 knots.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level Pacific low located along the southern California coast spread moisture into south central Arizona on September 11th. The moisture, combined with an unstable airmass, allowed scattered strong thunderstorms to develop during the afternoon and evening hours. Damaging winds in excess of 50 mph, dense blowing dust and large hail were all reported with these thunderstorms. A dust storm was generated across portions of the greater Phoenix metropolitan area during the early evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms to the south of the greater Phoenix area produced gusty outflow winds which stirred up significant dust and generated a dust storm which moved into the central and eastern portions of the greater Phoenix area during the early evening hours on September 11th. Multiple trained weather spotters reported visibility at or below one quarter mile in dense blowing dust...including a spotter in Laveen at 7th Avenue and Interstate 10. The blowing dust was a significant hazard to motorists during the latter stages of the rush hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistent upper level Pacific low pressure system located along the southern California coast led to scattered thunderstorms over southwest Arizona during the afternoon and evening hours on September 12th. Some of the storms produced strong and gusty winds which produced a dust storm in the Yuma area. Large hail was also generated by the stronger storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Isolated strong thunderstorms developed in the Yuma area during the afternoon hours on September 12th, and they produced gusty and damaging microburst winds. According to the Park manager at 5201 E US highway 95, strong winds blew down two big trees which fell on a large building full of equipment and stored materials. This caused an estimated 30,000 dollars of damage to the building, fencing and equipment. In addition, the front sign that showed where trailers were located was damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm dropped heavy rain over the area that was burned by the Schultz Fire.  Heavy runoff produced flash flooding below the burned area in Hutchinson Acres and along Campbell Avenue.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding was reported along Campbell Avenue.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with large hail moved through the Western fringes of the Phoenix metropolitan area during the afternoon hours on September 11th. A trained weather spotter at the Palo Verde fire station near Wintersburg reported hail varying from pea-sized to silver dollar sized. The largest hail reported was estimated to be nearly one and one half inch in diameter. No damage was reported associated with the large hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High temperatures on Sunday, September 11th reached near or slightly above 100 degrees in the greater Phoenix area. A hiker from Missouri attempted to hike South Mountain during the late morning, but ran out of water and became lost. He died early in the afternoon as a result of heatstroke and dehydration.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On the morning of September 11th, a 31 year old Missouri man and his friend decided to hike up South Mountain. The Missouri man was in town to watch the ASU/Missouri football game on Saturday the 10th. The two men went hiking in South Mountain Preserve Sunday morning and left with both water and a map. According to Scott Walker of the Phoenix Fire Department, they got lost and ran out of water at approximately 11 am and became disoriented. The hikers were deep in the mountains when a 12:40 pm emergency call was made. The Missouri man was found dead at 2 pm and rescuers indicated he died from heat stroke and dehydration. The second man survived and did not need hospitalization. The high temperature at Phoenix on Sunday was 99 degrees and some of the hotter deserts reached triple digits.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced heavy rain over the Wallow Burn Area which flooded roads south of Alpine.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain fell for about 45 minutes with a total of 0.65 in Alpine.  Several County/Forest Service Roads and Highway 180 were flooded south of Alpine. One road was closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Wind from a thunderstorm caused damage in Chino Valley. Heavy rain (1.70) from the thunderstorm also caused flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind from a thunderstorm knocked down several trees.  The trees were up to 18 inches in diameter.  A few septic storage tanks about 15 feet in diameter blew across Highway 89.  One vehicle was damaged by the blowing tanks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Wind from a thunderstorm caused damage in Chino Valley. Heavy rain (1.70) from the thunderstorm also caused flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few side streets were impassible in Chino Valley due to heavy rain from a thunderstorm. Highway 89 had four inches of standing water on it at times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced heavy rain that caused flash flooding in Antelope Canyon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tuba City Police received a report that there was a high volume of water flowing through Antelope Canyon. The water flowed for 9 hours in this normally dry slot canyon. Five feet of sand washed out of the upper portion of the canyon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from thunderstorms caused flash flooding in the Wallow Fire area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A flash flood and mud slide crossed Highway 191 just south of Alpine.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rain caused flash flooding in Matkatamiba Canyon,  a side canyon to the Grand Canyon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from a thunderstorm caused a flash flood in Matkatamiba Canyon.  A group of rafters was hiking in the side canyon of the Grand Canyon when a 13 foot wall of water came crashing through.  They were trapped in a wide spot in the canyon above a narrow spot (3 to 5 feet wide) where the flood water was raging 35 to 40 feet deep. Their boats were tied at the mouth of Matkatamiba Canyon.  Two of their boats flipped when the flood hit. Other boats broke loose and floated down the Colorado River and were recovered later.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced heavy rain over the eastern rim country north of Payson.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The remote weather station on Promontory Point picked up 2.97 inches of rain in less than two hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Elevated moisture and weakly unstable air led to cumulus clouds with virga over the Mojave Desert.  One virga shaft penetrated down into the extremely hot and dry layer of air below, producing high winds at the surface as it evaporated.
Heavy rain from thunderstorms produced flash flooding and mudslides around Alpine.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain over Escudilla Peak area caused flash flooding and mud slides over sections of Highway 180/191 north and southeast of Alpine. Mud, trees, and rocks temporarily blocked the highway between Alpine and Nutrioso.  The Alpine Ranger District Office experienced minor flooding in front of the office. Flooding and erosion on private property was also reported.  Rainfall amounts of around an inch to one and a half inches was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rain produced flash flooding below the Schultz Fire area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain (0.28 to 0.98) over the Schultz Fire area caused flash flooding in several of the neighborhoods below the burned area. Several roads were covered with water (up to 12) and debris.  These roads were closed until they were cleared.  Some private driveways were destroyed.

CZ_NAME: WHITE MOUNTAINS OF GRAHAM AND GREENLEE COUNTIES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Long term drought conditions continued during September with some improvement during the beginning of the month, mostly across eastern Pima County. Dry conditions returned the later half of the month as the Monsoon began to wind down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The month of September started off with above average rainfall across eastern Pima County and well as western Cochise County. However, the rest of the area continued to receive below normal rainfall and above normal temperatures. Tucson International Airport received the fifth highest rainfall amount in recorded history on September 15 of 2.84 inches. However, locations nearby received a tenth of an inch or less. This allowed the Tucson area, and northeast Pima County to be dropped from extreme drought conditions (D3) to severe drought conditions (D2). Extreme drought condition areas were lowered by almost 4 percent by the end of the month. However, due to the lack of rainfall across eastern Cochise, Graham, and Greenlee Counties, exceptional drought conditions (D4) expanded across southern Greenlee County. Most areas received less than 50 percent of the average rainfall, while areas in eastern Pima County and western Cochise County received greater than 300 percent of normal. Drought impacts across the state actually lessened in September as dry conditions have continued for several months with little change from month to month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Favorable positioning of the monsoon high pressure system over New Mexico allowed for a surge of moisture into southern Arizona. An upper level disturbance rotating around the high was the focus for thunderstorm development across the region. Some of the storms produced damaging winds, heavy rainfall, and flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained weather spotter recorded wind gusts of up to 76 mph in the Catalina Foothills area which resulted in considerable damage. Several homes received minor roof damage and broken windows and one home had its air conditioning unit blown off. Several mesquite trees were also uprooted and a brick wall was blown over near Harrison Road and Snyder Road. Some of the larger trees fell onto homes but caused only minor damage.

An upper level disturbance rotating around the high was the focus for thunderstorm development across the region. Some of the storms produced damaging winds, heavy rainfall, and flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a gas station in Catalina while a tanker was off-loading diesel into a 10,000 gallon tank. The strike blew open two manhole covers causing fuel vapors to ignite. The area was evacuated while the vapors quickly burned out.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed several downed power poles near the Tucson International Airport. This caused the closure of East Los Reales Road between South Sixth Avenue and South Nogales Highway and 3,200 customers to lose power.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tohono O'odham Nation Emergency Managers reported a car being struck by lightning on Highway 86 near Indian Route 34.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was sighted from the Arizona Department of Transportation yard in Grey Mountain.

This allowed the Tucson area, and northeast Pima County to be dropped from extreme drought conditions (D3) to severe drought conditions (D2). Extreme drought condition areas were lowered by almost 4 percent by the end of the month. However, due to the lack of rainfall across eastern Cochise, Graham, and Greenlee Counties, exceptional drought conditions (D4) expanded across southern Greenlee County. Most areas received less than 50 percent of the average rainfall, while areas in eastern Pima County and western Cochise County received greater than 300 percent of normal. Drought impacts across the state actually lessened in September as dry conditions have continued for several months with little change from month to month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Long term drought conditions continued in August although minor improvement due to beneficial monsoon rain, occurred across parts of Cochise, Graham and Greenlee Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After beneficial rainfall in July and early August, the entire month of August ended with below normal rainfall except across parts of western Cochise County. The result was that D3 extreme drought conditions retreated eastward, completely exiting Santa Cruz County, by the middle of the month, but expanded even farther westward across Pima and Pinal Counties by the end of August. D4 exceptional drought conditions did show an overall decrease in state coverage from 4.83% to 1.35%, with only extreme eastern Cochise County, southern Greenlee County, and extreme southeast Graham County remaining in exceptional drought conditions in southeast Arizona. The most impacted watersheds during August were the San Simon watershed (2nd percentile for rainfall) and the Santa Cruz watershed (2nd percentile for rainfall), which received very little rainfall. July's rainfall brought an end to the fire season so many recreational areas were reopened in August. The state and federal forest services conducted large scale seeding operations across the burn scar areas attempting to mitigate the flash flooding concerns. Very few ranchers were still holding onto cattle, so agriculture impacts were somewhat lessened during the month. Cotton and vegetable crops were reported as being fair to excellent as irrigation was being maximized.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tohono O'odham Nation dispatch reported thunderstorm winds downed several power poles down along Highway 85 between Why and Ajo.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Increasing moisture along the New Mexico and Arizona border, along with an upper level disturbance, provided a focus for thunderstorm development across southeast Arizona. Some of the storms produced heavy rainfall, strong winds, and frequent lightning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall amounts up to 2.5 inches resulted in flash flooding in the Kino Springs area. Several washes and the Santa Cruz River flooded resulting in the closure of several local roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Increasing moisture along the New Mexico and Arizona border, along with an upper level disturbance, provided a focus for thunderstorm development across southeast Arizona. Some of the storms produced heavy rainfall, strong winds, and frequent lightning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two illegal immigrants were struck by lightning near Sierra Vista and transported to a hospital. The Border Patrol reported that the injuries sustained by the two illegal immigrants was consistent with a lightning strike.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Increasing moisture along the New Mexico and Arizona border, along with an upper level disturbance, provided a focus for thunderstorm development across southeast Arizona. Some of the storms produced heavy rainfall, strong winds, and frequent lightning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts over 60 mph resulted in minor damage in Fort Thomas. Several truck trailers were blown through yards resulting in a few outbuildings being destroyed. A tool shed was also severely damaged and a few small trees were downed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abundant moisture and a mesoscale convective vortex from overnight storms in Sonora, Mexico were the focus for thunderstorm development. Some of the storms produced strong winds and heavy rainfall, including a microburst in Marana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall amounts of 1 to 2 inches in less than 20 minutes resulted in widespread flash flooding near the Dove Mountain Community and Marana. Several streets were impassable for nearly an hour as water rushed through flooded washes and ditches. Notable road closures included Tangerine Road and Camino de Manana.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abundant moisture and a mesoscale convective vortex from overnight storms in Sonora, Mexico were the focus for thunderstorm development. Some of the storms produced strong winds and heavy rainfall, including a microburst in Marana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall amounts of 1 to 2 inches resulted in flash flooding and several road closures across the Marana area. Flooding washes and ditches resulted in the closure of Cortaro Road from Thornydale Road to Interstate 10. Several additional neighborhood roads were flooded.

A weak low pressure system off the Baja Peninsula also helped to initiate scattered showers and thunderstorms across southern Arizona. Some of the storms produced heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall in excess of one inch under one hour on the Monument Fire burn area resulted in flooding along Montezuma Canyon Road. Heavy rainfall also resulted in water rushing across and down Hereford Road making the road impassable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abundant moisture, favorable flow, and an outflow boundary from storms over Sonora, Mexico resulted in scattered showers and thunderstorms. Some of the storms produced heavy rainfall and strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall in the Benson area resulted in several overflowing washes and closed neighborhood streets.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall resulted in zero visibility along Interstate 10 near Ina Road resulting in an accident. A tractor trailer drove into the median and flipped as a result on the heavy rainfall. The driver sustained minor injuries and was treated on scene.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cochise County Emergency Manager reported water across Highway 90 at several locations from Sierra Vista to Bisbee.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter recorded a wind gust of 62 mph at his home near Patagonia Lake.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall amounts in excess of 2 inches resulted in flash flooding across portions of the northeast Tucson metro area including the foothills of the Santa Catalina Mountains. One motorist had to be rescued at the intersection near Fort Lowell Road and Melpomene Way. Water reached the top of the car, trapping him inside. In addition, several rock slides were reported along Mount Lemmon Highway due to runoff from the heavy rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abundant moisture and favorable flow allowed for thunderstorms that produced heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall over the Monument Fire burn area resulted in flooding in Miller Canyon and the closure of Miller Canyon Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abundant moisture and favorable flow allowed for thunderstorms that produced heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall lead to a quick five foot rise in the Nogales Wash and subsequent flooding along its banks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed over the south central deserts of Arizona during the afternoon on October 4th, and they produced strong gusty outflow winds. Due to the very dry conditions across the deserts, the winds were able to generated dense blowing dust which led to dust storm conditions. The near zero visibilities caused a series of significant traffic accidents along the Interstate 10 corridor from Tucson to Casa Grande. The most significant accident, which involved 16 vehicles and produced 2 fatalites, occurred near Picacho Peak. Another major accident occurred near the community of Casa Grande.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed over the south central deserts during the early afternoon hours on October 4th. The storms produced gusty outflow winds; due to very dry conditions the winds generated dust storm conditions in the Casa Grande area. According to the Department of Highways, a major accident occurred near 240 pm, which involved eight vehicles. The accident was located at milepost 190, about 4 miles north of Casa Grande on Interstate 10. No fatalities were reported, but there were two serious, yet non life-threatening injuries. East bound lanes of Interstate 10 were closed for an hour due to the accident. Additionally, there were two other crashes later in the day which likely were the result of dust. A crash involving two tractor-trailers and a small car on Interstate 10 near the Gila River Indian Community south of Phoenix seriously injured two people.  Another crash occurred on Interstate 10 near Tonopah, but it did not involve serious injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure located over southern California interacted with increasing moisture across southern Arizona to produce strong to severe afternoon and evening thunderstorms. Some of the storms produced heavy rainfall, strong winds, and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall resulted in several flooded washes and ditches which closed streets.  A swift water rescue was performed on Wilmot north of Sahuarita Road. Firefighters worked to rescue a stranded women from her car but were forced to walk to her as high water was blocking the roadway. Additionally, a wall of water rushed down the Pantano Wash catching two kids off guard. Firefighters had to rescue the kids from waist high water. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms produced strong winds across Arizona. These strong winds interacted with very dry soils resulting in isolated dust storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a palm tree in the 3400 block of South Camino Seco in Tucson igniting the tree. Debris then fell onto a mobile home, setting the home on fire. The occupants were able to safely escape. Only minor damage was reported to the roof of the home.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong overnight complex in Sonora, Mexico produced an outflow boundary and mesoscale convective vortex that tracked north across southern Arizona. This feature was the focus of strong to severe thunderstorms, including a strong microburst in Eloy.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds up to 75 mph impacted Eloy, Arizona when a microburst descended near the Volvo equipment rental facility. These strong winds resulted in significant damage in a divergent pattern across southeast portions of Eloy. A total of 20 homes sustained significant damage. Some had roof structures blown off, while some suffered total roof collapses and one had a tree fell on it and a nearby vehicle. Of the 20 homes, 14 were condemned. Several other homes sustained damage due to flying debris while still others only had shingle damage. In addition, eight businesses were damaged beyond repair including one that also collapsed onto cars and equipment. Several power poles were also downed resulting in widespread power outages in the August heat forcing Eloy to set up a cooling center in a local high school. A number of semi-tractor trailers were also blown over in a parking lot. One person sustained minor injuries when winds ripped the roof off a mobile home.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abundant moisture, favorable southeasterly flow aloft, and terrain influences resulted in isolated showers and thunderstorms across southeast Arizona. Some of the storms produced heavy rainfall and strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed power poles on Central Highway between Latimer Road and Hopkins Road in Elfrida.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abundant moisture, favorable southeasterly flow aloft, and terrain influences resulted in isolated showers and thunderstorms across southeast Arizona. Some of the storms produced heavy rainfall and strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall resulted in flash flooding along Central Highway in McNeal. Law Enforcment reported three cars stranded in rushing water at the intersection of Davis Road and Central Highway with two additional cars swept down Davis Road. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mesoscale convective vortex from overnight convection in Sonora, Mexico acted as a focus for isolated showers and thunderstorms. A few of the storms produced strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts of 52 knots (60 mph) resulted in three power poles downed along Route 15 between Santa Rose and the Pinal County line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening hours on September 24th, isolated showers and weak thunderstorms developed over portions of the central deserts, including the greater Phoenix area. Due to the very dry lower atmosphere, showers and virga in the area generated strong and gusty surface winds; the winds were sub-severe but they were still strong enough to cause damage to power lines in central Phoenix.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Isolated to scattered showers developed across the central Arizona deserts during the evening hours on September 24th. No lightning was reported in the greater Phoenix area, however the very dry lower atmosphere allowed strong gusty winds to develop near the showers and associated virga. A local utility company reported that power lines were downed on Sherman Street between 19th Avenue and 23rd Avenue, and resulted in the loss of power to 1100 customers. The damaging winds were sub severe and according to radar estimates and surrounding observations ranged from 40 to 45 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Increasing moisture, an upper level disturbance, and unusually hot temperatures created a very unstable environment that led to scattered afternoon and evening thunderstorms. Some of the storms produced strong winds and heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms winds downed trees and power poles in Rio Rico along Interstate 19.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abundant moisture and a mesoscale convective vortex from overnight storms in Sonora, Mexico were the focus for thunderstorm development. Some of the storms produced strong winds and heavy rainfall, including a microburst in Marana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms winds in Marana downed numerous trees from the Dove Mountain Community to Continental Reserve. A few trees were downed on homes with only minor damage sustained to roofs. The most significant damage occurred along Ina Road where 18 power poles were downed. Some poles fell on businesses and fences, and one fell on a moving commercial vehicle. Ina Road was closed for several hours, effectively isolating people in area businesses, parking lots and homes from the rest of the community.

An upper level disturbance rotating around the high was the focus for thunderstorm development across the region. Some of the storms produced damaging winds, heavy rainfall, and flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall amounts between 1.5 inches and 2.5 inches resulted flooding and the closure of  several roads at Organ Pipe National Monument. In addition, a 40 foot section of the international border fence between the United States and Mexico was destroyed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure center over northern Arizona and abundant moisture brought thunderstorms with heavy rain and severe weather to the northern portions of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained weather spotter reported a funnel cloud off of Highway 89A north of Flagstaff (three miles east of Milepost 440 on Highway 89A).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong overnight complex in Sonora, Mexico produced an outflow boundary and mesoscale convective vortex that tracked north across southern Arizona. This feature was the focus of strong to severe thunderstorms, including a strong microburst in Eloy.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed several power poles along Sunland Gin Road between Harmon Road and Pretzer Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Decreasing moisture and weak flow confined thunderstorms to the higher terrain of southeast Arizona. Some of the storms produced brief strong winds and heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The highest of several measured thunderstorm gusts in excess of 60 mph ripped a roof off a barn in Canelo.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Decreasing moisture and weak flow confined thunderstorms to the higher terrain of southeast Arizona. Some of the storms produced brief strong winds and heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall resulted in flash flooding along the San Francisco River in southern Clifton. The flooding was confined to Ward Canyon where a few homes sustained minor water damage along Ward Canyon Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ample moisture and favorable flow allowed for a few thunderstorms across southern Arizona. While most storms remained below severe limits, storms still produced strong winds and heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds damaged homes and downed power lines from Big Fields to Covered Wells on the Tohono O'odham Nation. Four homes incurred roof damage and one power pole was downed in Big Field. Three power poles were also downed in Covered Wells along Highway 86 which resulted in power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system in northern Arizona interacted with abundant moisture to produce showers and thunderstorms across southern Arizona. Some of the storms produced large hail, torrential rains, and strong winds. The Tucson International Airport recorded the fifth wettest day in recorded history.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The fifth highest daily rainfall total in recorded history occurred at the Tucson International Airport. A total of 2.84 inches of rain fell at the airport with other amounts up to 3 inches in the area. Significant flash flooding with major damage, several swift water rescues, rivers flooding their banks, and several flooded structures were a result of this heavy rainfall. A total of 6 swift water rescues were conducted and at least six people were rescued from flooded homes on Calle Medina. At the peak of the storm, nearly 3 feet of water covered most of the roads in and around Tucson International Airport with over 30 roads being closed and several more impassible. Two flights were also detoured to Phoenix Sky Harbor Airport. Several homes and businesses were flooded as well. A Jiffy Lube had water get into the oil pit causing the oil pit to overflow into the streets and washes around the business creating a HAZMAT situation. Several businesses were closed for the remainder of the day while repairs and cleanup occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms produced strong winds across Arizona. These strong winds interacted with very dry soils resulting in isolated dust storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds kicked up an area of dust along Interstate 10 near Picacho Peak, dropping visibility to near zero. One driver slowed enough to cause a tractor trailer to rear end the vehicle setting off a 13 vehicle accident. In all there were 12 injuries with 5 people being transported to local hospitals.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds produced an area of blowing dust across southern Pinal County that resulted in a deadly traffic accident.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A fiery ten vehicle accident killed an infant and closed westbound lanes of Interstate 10 near Red Rock. The cause of the accident was zero visibility due to a dust storm. The infant was in a car seat in the backseat of a car that apparently slowed down when it entered the dust and was struck in the rear by a semi-tractor trailer. The driver also received serious injuries and an older child who was a passenger received minor injuries. Both were transported to the hospital. Westbound lanes remained closed for several hours following the accident.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Adequate moisture and terrain influences allowed for isolated afternoon and evening thunderstorms across southern Arizona, some of which produced strong winds and heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds caused significant structural damage to a home and damaged an outhouse in South Komelik.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Increasing moisture and favorable upper level flow allowed for isolated to scattered afternoon and evening thunderstorms. A few of the storms produced strong winds.

Favorable positioning of the monsoon high pressure system over New Mexico allowed for a surge of moisture into southern Arizona. An upper level disturbance rotating around the high was the focus for thunderstorm development across the region. Some of the storms produced damaging winds, heavy rainfall, and flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts uprooted up to two dozen trees at the Oro Valley Marketplace shopping center.

CZ_NAME: UPPER SANTA CRUZ RIVER AND ALTAR VALLEYS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Long term drought conditions continued in August although minor improvement due to beneficial monsoon rain, occurred across parts of Cochise, Graham and Greenlee Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After beneficial rainfall in July and early August, the entire month of August ended with below normal rainfall except across parts of western Cochise County. The result was that D3 extreme drought conditions retreated eastward, completely exiting Santa Cruz County, by the middle of the month, but expanded even farther westward across Pima and Pinal Counties by the end of August. D4 exceptional drought conditions did show an overall decrease in state coverage from 4.83% to 1.35%, with only extreme eastern Cochise County, southern Greenlee County, and extreme southeast Graham County remaining in exceptional drought conditions in southeast Arizona. The most impacted watersheds during August were the San Simon watershed (2nd percentile for rainfall) and the Santa Cruz watershed (2nd percentile for rainfall), which received very little rainfall. July's rainfall brought an end to the fire season so many recreational areas were reopened in August. The state and federal forest services conducted large scale seeding operations across the burn scar areas attempting to mitigate the flash flooding concerns. Very few ranchers were still holding onto cattle, so agriculture impacts were somewhat lessened during the month. Cotton and vegetable crops were reported as being fair to excellent as irrigation was being maximized.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Long term drought conditions continued during the month of June, expanding in coverage and intensity as southern Arizona received little to no rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extremely dry conditions continued in June with no rainfall to begin the month. The first Drought Monitor issued during June expanded D3 conditions to include all of Graham, Greenlee, and Cochise Counties. It also introduced the first D4 exceptional drought condition into extreme eastern Cochise County and extreme southern Greenlee County. The D4 drought areas had received no significant rainfall since October, 2010. The last measurable rainfall at Bisbee-Douglas International Airport was on February 27, 2011. The airport finally received two hundredths of an inch on June 29 bringing an end to 122 consecutive days without measurable rainfall. The Tucson Airport also went 81 days with no measurable rainfall, making it the fourth longest dry spell on record. ||The large Wallow Fire and Horseshoe 2 Fire prompted the U.S. Forest Service to close Coronado National Forest on June 10. However, a new fire, the Monument Fire, started on June 12 in the Huachuca Mountains that threatened the city of Sierra Vista. By the end of the month, these three massive fires had charred more than three-quarter of a million acres. ||The Arizona Drought Watch continued to report serious watershed conditions all across southern Arizona in June. Unusually low water levels in reservoirs, lakes, and ponds with unusually low flows in streams rivers, and springs. This resulted in unusually low forage and vegetation range productivity, along with poor forage nutrition and increased toxicity of forage. Due to drought-related wildfires, hundreds of thousands of acres of range land forest was lost. Agriculture impacts included limited cattle grazing which in turn caused a leaner herd that needed to be sent to market prematurely because of a lack of food and water sources. Tourism was also impacted from the closure of national and state parks due to wildfire concerns. Native reptile and amphibian species were also harmed as a result of dried up ponds and streams.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe to extreme drought conditions continued through the month of April across southeast Arizona.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dry conditions continued during the month of April across much of Arizona as only one significant winter storm moved through the state. Portions of southeast Cochise County received no measurable rainfall. Portions of south central Arizona received amounts from one tenth to one half inch while other areas, including eastern mountain ranges, received one tenth of an inch or less. More than 27,000 acres in Arizona have been consumed by wildfires through the end of April, mostly in the southern part of the state. Little change in the D3 condition area with all of Cochise and Santa Cruz Counties under D3 conditions. Portions of southern Greenlee, southern Graham, and eastern Pima were also still under D3 conditions. ||Arizona Drought Watch reported several impacts during the month. The Santa Cruz River basin of eastern Pima county reported unusually low flows in streams, rivers, and springs. Low soil moisture was resulting in plant stress, newly observed plant mortality, and oak leaves browning and falling with only 25% of Oaks flowering. Other unusual plant phenology was also observed with pines not producing cones and most flowers not budding at all. Dead oaks and ponderosas were quickly rotting at the base and falling within several months.||In the San Pedro River Basin in northern Cochise and southern Graham Counties, Arizona Drought Watch reported plant stress due to unusually low soil moisture. Native plants, including cacti and cotton plants are stressed, or have died.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms developed over the central Arizona deserts during the late afternoon and evening hours on July 31st. Initially the storms produced a dust storm that moved from northwest Pinal county into the Phoenix metropolitan area. The dust storm was followed by additional thunderstorms that brought damaging microburst winds and heavy rains to the central deserts including the greater Phoenix area. Damaging microburst winds also affected the area around Casa Grande.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained weather spotter reported that there were numerous power poles downed just south of Gilbert Road and Hunt highway, about 5 miles east of Sun Lakes. The poles were downed by strong microburst winds estimated to be at least 60 knots. Strong microburst winds from these storms also blew over a nearby block wall approximately 10 feet long and 6 feet tall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Continued dry conditions and fuels allowed for the Horseshoe 2 fire to burn through much of the month of June. Full containment of the fire was reached on June 25 after the 222,954 acres were consumed representing 80 percent of the Chriricahua Mountains. The estimated containment cost was $51 million.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Horseshoe 2 fire continued to burn through much of the month of June as extremely dry conditions and fuels, and strong winds aided in the fire's growth. As the fire continued to grow to the northwest, the communities of Cave Creek Canyon, Paradise, Whitetail Canyon, West Turkey Creek, and West Pinery Canyon were evacuated. Crews were put in place to protect the communities, but some structures were lost. In all, 9 homes and 14 outbuildings burned, including the historic Barfoot fire lookout. Chiricahua National Monument in the northern portion of the fire was closed on June 3 and remained closed through the summer. ||One firefighter was injured when he and another lookout were caught between two burnouts. The firefighter required several stitches in his hand after using it to break a window for access to a rock shelter in order to escape the rapidly approaching flames. The fire passed over and into the shelter, but no injuries directly attributed to the fire occurred. However, this indirect injury, when added to the 12 suffered in May, brought the total to 13 firefighters indirectly injured fighting the Horseshoe 2 Fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several strong thunderstorms developed to the southeast of the greater Phoenix area during the evening hours on July 5. The storms produced damaging microburst winds and dense blowing dust that affected portions of central and western Pinal county. Gusty winds estimated to be in excess of 50 knots uprooted trees, knocked down power poles and blew a semi truck off of the interstate.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms with damaging microburst winds affected the community of Sacaton during the evening hours on July 5th.  According to Pinal county Emergency Managers, gusty winds estimated to be around 65 knots uprooted two twenty inch trees. The two trees then fell on the police station located at the Gila River indian community. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Increasing moisture and a mesoscale convective vortex from overnight thunderstorms in Sonora helped trigger strong to severe thunderstorms across eastern Arizona.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm outflows raced through the Bisbee-Douglas Airport resulting in a measured wind gust of 56 knots. No damage or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another push of monsoon moisture fueled more thunderstorms across the Mojave Desert and southern Great Basin. Several storms produced flash flooding, and there was also isolated severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain flooded one basement in Colorado City.

CZ_NAME: UPPER SANTA CRUZ RIVER AND ALTAR VALLEYS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extreme drought conditions and strong winds caused a human-started fire to quickly become out of control. A separate fire, the Pajarita Fire, started in early June and later combined with the Murphy Fire. The two fires were known as the Murphy Complex Wildfire which persisted into mid June and consumed over 68,000 acres.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme drought conditions coupled with strong winds resulted in a human caused fire getting out of control and spreading into rugged terrain of Santa Cruz county. The fire was started on May 30 and continued through mid June. A total of 68,078 acres of grass, shrub, oak, and mesquite burned. Two structures were lost including the historic Atascosa Lookout in the Atascosa Mountains and an outhouse at Pena Blanca Lake. The estimated total cost of resources devoted to fighting the wildfire was $5.5 million.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe thunderstorm development had occured in the early afternoon hours of July 5th near the Tucson, AZ area and east of Tucson. As thunderstorms dissipated and began moving northwest, outflows from these storms began producing very strong downburst winds, some in excess of 70 mph. Around 6:30pm MST, the NWS Phoenix office began receiving calls of dust moving into the Casa Grande, AZ area, about 50 miles southeast of downtown Phoenix. Upon examining radar data, it is estimated the dust storm reached a maximum height of 5000 feet to 6000 feet, the aerial coverage was close to 100 miles and the distance traveled was around 150 miles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms to the southeast of the greater Phoenix area generated microburst winds that moved to the northwest and into the Phoenix metropolitan area. A trained weather spotter at Gilbert and Riggs Road measured a peak wind gust of 69 mph. Other spotter reports in the area reported winds estimated between 50 and 65 mph. The official weather station at Chandler measured a 50 knot gust at 8 pm, and another 50 knot gust was recorded by the Goodyear sensor a 847 pm. The winds generated dense blowing dust, and damaged a number of trees. 10 trees were uprooted or damaged at the intersection of Arizona Avenue and Chandler Boulevard. Downed trees were also reported long Ivanhoe road in west Chandler, Chandler Heights road, and Pleasant Drive and Alma School roads. Additionally,  the strong winds at Chandler airport blew a plane loose from its moorings and caused it to flip over. The flipped plane then blew into a nearby chain link fence and damaged 100 feet of the barrier.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure area slowly moving through the  Great Basin and Colorado brought snow to northern Arizona. Over a foot of snow fell over portions of the Mogollon Rim where the upslope flow enhanced the snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell over the White Mountains area.  Two feet of snow was reported at the  Show Low airport and eight inches fell in McNary.
