EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms developed over southeast Arizona during the afternoon hours on July 18 and they moved northwestward into the central deserts. Strong gusty winds from the thunderstorms produced a series of dust storms which affected the greater Phoenix metropolitan area as well as the communities of Coolidge and Casa Grande. Weather spotters reported visibilities down to near zero miles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong thunderstorm moved across Youngtown during the early evening hours on July 18 and generated gusty winds in excess of 50 mph. A trained weather spotter reported marble sized hail ranging from one half to one inch in diameter. Visibility was lowered to around one mile in blowing dust and some tree branches were damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Favorable positioning of upper level high pressure brought southeast flow and a corresponding increase in moisture which resulted in afternoon and evening thunderstorms across southern Arizona. Some of the storms produced strong winds.

CZ_NAME: UPPER SANTA CRUZ RIVER AND ALTAR VALLEYS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Long term drought continued into March resulting in an upgrade to D3 conditions across southeast Arizona.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Abnormally dry conditions continued during the month of March. The southeast quarter of the state received less than 25% of average rainfall year-to-date and less than 50% for the water year (October to September). This lack of rainfall resulted in an upgrade from D2 conditions to D3 conditions across much of Cochise County on March 8. Later in the month on March 29, all of Cochise County as well as Santa Cruz County, southern Graham County, southern Greenlee County, and eastern Pima County were upgraded to D3 conditions. Prolonged drought conditions helped worsen critical fire weather conditions which helped contribute to numerous small brush fires. The Arizona Drought Watch report for extreme southeast Cochise County, including the Rio Bavispe watershed, found many ponds that normally have water during March were dry. Also, intermittent streams that normally flow were dry in many or all segments. Little to no green forage existed in the grasses or forbs, and pipelines and troughs have replaced most pond water resulting in significant agriculture impacts. The Arizona Drought Watch report for extreme southeast Pima County, including the Santa Cruz River watershed, found that drought impacts are present and severe. The flow in the Santa Cruz is less than last year and about half the average flow seen within drought years. Rising severity was also observed. Water levels were approximately two feet lower than last year and approxiamately six feet lower than pre-drought levels.

CZ_NAME: UPPER SANTA CRUZ RIVER AND ALTAR VALLEYS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Long term drought conditions continued during the month of June, expanding in coverage and intensity as southern Arizona received little to no rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extremely dry conditions continued in June with no rainfall to begin the month. The first Drought Monitor issued during June expanded D3 conditions to include all of Graham, Greenlee, and Cochise Counties. It also introduced the first D4 exceptional drought condition into extreme eastern Cochise County and extreme southern Greenlee County. The D4 drought areas had received no significant rainfall since October, 2010. The last measurable rainfall at Bisbee-Douglas International Airport was on February 27, 2011. The airport finally received two hundredths of an inch on June 29 bringing an end to 122 consecutive days without measurable rainfall. The Tucson Airport also went 81 days with no measurable rainfall, making it the fourth longest dry spell on record. ||The large Wallow Fire and Horseshoe 2 Fire prompted the U.S. Forest Service to close Coronado National Forest on June 10. However, a new fire, the Monument Fire, started on June 12 in the Huachuca Mountains that threatened the city of Sierra Vista. By the end of the month, these three massive fires had charred more than three-quarter of a million acres. ||The Arizona Drought Watch continued to report serious watershed conditions all across southern Arizona in June. Unusually low water levels in reservoirs, lakes, and ponds with unusually low flows in streams rivers, and springs. This resulted in unusually low forage and vegetation range productivity, along with poor forage nutrition and increased toxicity of forage. Due to drought-related wildfires, hundreds of thousands of acres of range land forest was lost. Agriculture impacts included limited cattle grazing which in turn caused a leaner herd that needed to be sent to market prematurely because of a lack of food and water sources. Tourism was also impacted from the closure of national and state parks due to wildfire concerns. Native reptile and amphibian species were also harmed as a result of dried up ponds and streams.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Favorable positioning of upper level high pressure brought southeast flow and a corresponding increase in moisture which resulted in afternoon and evening thunderstorms across southern Arizona. Some of the storms produced strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain fell across portions of the Tucson Metro on July 4 with rainfall amounts ranging from 1 to 2.5 inches. The heaviest rains were reported in the Catalina Foothills and across eastern Tucson. This resulted in the Pantano Wash flooding several surface streets and low water crossings in southeast Tucson, including the closure of East Irvington at Houghton Road. Additional street closures included the 6500 block of Old Spanish Trail at Rincon Creek on the east side and Camino De La Tierra at the Rillito Wash in midtown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with strong, damaging microburst winds developed across the central deserts during the late afternoon and evening hours on August 3rd. The storms affected much of the greater Phoenix metropolitan area, and brought locally heavy rain as well as winds in excess of 50 mph. The gusty winds also produced areas of dense blowing dust in locations such as Gila Bend.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms in the greater Phoenix area produced strong and damaging wind gusts during the evening hours on August 3rd. A trained weather spotter reported that a large mesquite tree was downed near the 303 and Greenway road. Wind gusts were estimated to be in excess of 50 knots.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abundant moisture, favorable monsoonal flow, and an outflow boundary from Sonora, Mexico allowed for isolated to scattered showers and thunderstorms along the International Border. A few storms produced strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained weather spotter near Tubac estimated a thunderstorm wind gust of 60 mph at her home.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from thunderstorms produced flash flooding near Greer and Alpine.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Apache Country Road 1126 near Greer flooded. There was over an inch of standing water in the Greer Fire Station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced large hail and a funnel cloud over Chino Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was sighted west of Chino Valley.
